skills.title: Create a new Next.js application with create-next-app
skills.summary: Scaffold a new Next.js project using create-next-app with App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and recommended defaults.
skills.declaration: I will create a new Next.js project using `npx create-next-app@latest` with App Router, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS enabled. The project will use the `src/` directory and default import alias `@/*`. If you want Pages Router, JavaScript, or a different configuration, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Create a New Next.js Application ### 1. Pre-flight checks - Verify Node.js 18.18+ is installed: run `node -v`. If below 18.18, stop and upgrade first. - Verify npm or another package manager is available: `npm -v` (or `pnpm -v`, `yarn -v`, `bun -v`). - Confirm the target directory does not already contain a `package.json` — do NOT scaffold inside an existing project unless the user explicitly wants a monorepo sub-app. - If a directory with the desired project name already exists, warn the user before proceeding. ### 2. Run create-next-app - Run the scaffolding command with recommended defaults: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest \ --typescript \ --tailwind \ --eslint \ --app \ --src-dir \ --import-alias "@/*" \ --use-npm ``` - Flag breakdown: - `--typescript`: Enables TypeScript (creates `.ts`/`.tsx` files, includes `tsconfig.json`). - `--tailwind`: Installs and configures Tailwind CSS. - `--eslint`: Sets up ESLint with Next.js recommended rules. - `--app`: Uses the App Router (`src/app/` directory) instead of Pages Router. - `--src-dir`: Places source code inside `src/` for cleaner project root. - `--import-alias "@/*"`: Configures `@/` as the import alias for `src/`. - `--use-npm`: Uses npm as the package manager. Replace with `--use-pnpm`, `--use-yarn`, or `--use-bun` based on user preference. - If the user has not specified a project name, ask for one. Do NOT use generic names like `my-app`. - Wait for the scaffolding to complete. It installs dependencies automatically. ### 3. Verify the scaffold succeeded - `cd` into the project directory. - Verify these files/directories exist: - `package.json` with `next`, `react`, `react-dom` in dependencies. - `tsconfig.json` with `paths: { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }`. - `next.config.ts` (or `next.config.mjs`). - `src/app/layout.tsx` — the root layout. - `src/app/page.tsx` — the home page. - `src/app/globals.css` — Tailwind CSS entry point. - `tailwind.config.ts` (v3) or `postcss.config.mjs` (v4, depending on create-next-app version). - `.eslintrc.json` (or `eslint.config.mjs` for flat config). - Verify `node_modules/` exists (dependencies were installed). - Check the installed Next.js version: `npx next --version`. ### 4. Start the development server - Run `npm run dev`. - Open `http://localhost:3000` in a browser. - Confirm the default Next.js welcome page loads without errors. - Check the terminal for any warnings or errors. ### 5. Clean up the default template - The scaffold includes demo content that should be replaced. Remove or simplify: - `src/app/page.tsx` — replace the entire default content with a minimal component: ```tsx export default function Home() { return (

Hello

); } ``` - `src/app/globals.css` — keep only the Tailwind import and CSS variable definitions. Remove the demo gradient styles. - Delete `public/next.svg` and `public/vercel.svg` if not needed. - Do NOT delete `src/app/layout.tsx`, `src/app/globals.css`, `next.config.ts`, or `tsconfig.json`. ### 6. Verify tsconfig.json paths - Open `tsconfig.json` and confirm `paths` is set: ```json { "compilerOptions": { "paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] } } } ``` - If `src/` directory is not used, paths should be `"@/*": ["./*"]`. - This alias is critical — most skills and libraries (shadcn/ui, Vitest, etc.) depend on `@/` resolving correctly. ### 7. Configure .gitignore - Verify `.gitignore` includes at minimum: - `node_modules/` - `.next/` - `.env*.local` - `*.tsbuildinfo` - `next-env.d.ts` - Add `.env` and `.env.local` if not already listed. - Add `coverage/` if tests will be added later. ### 8. Initialize git (if not already) - If the directory is not a git repo: `git init`. - Create an initial commit: `git add -A && git commit -m "init: scaffold Next.js project"`. - Do NOT push to a remote unless the user asks. ### 9. Optional: configure VS Code settings - If the project uses VS Code, create `.vscode/settings.json` with: ```json { "typescript.tsdk": "node_modules/typescript/lib", "editor.defaultFormatter": "esbenp.prettier-vscode", "editor.formatOnSave": true, "editor.codeActionsOnSave": { "source.fixAll.eslint": "explicit" } } ``` - Only create this if the user uses VS Code or does not specify an editor. ### 10. Project structure overview After cleanup, the project should look like: ``` / src/ app/ layout.tsx # Root layout (html, body, fonts, metadata) page.tsx # Home page globals.css # Tailwind imports + CSS variables public/ # Static assets next.config.ts # Next.js configuration tsconfig.json # TypeScript configuration with @/ alias tailwind.config.ts # Tailwind configuration (if v3) postcss.config.mjs # PostCSS configuration package.json .eslintrc.json # ESLint rules .gitignore ``` ### Common Errors & Fixes - **`npm ERR! code ENOENT` or `command not found: npx`** -- Node.js is not installed or not in PATH. Install Node.js 18.18+ from nodejs.org or via nvm: `nvm install 18`. - **`EACCES permission denied`** -- npm global directory has permission issues. Fix with `sudo chown -R $(whoami) $(npm config get prefix)/{lib/node_modules,bin,share}` or use nvm which avoids sudo entirely. - **Port 3000 already in use** -- Another process is using port 3000. Either kill it (`lsof -ti:3000 | xargs kill`) or start Next.js on a different port: `npm run dev -- -p 3001`. - **`Module not found: Can't resolve '@/...'`** -- The `@/*` path alias is misconfigured. Verify `tsconfig.json` has `paths: { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }` and the `src/` directory exists. If not using `src/`, set `"@/*": ["./*"]`. - **Tailwind classes not applying** -- Check that `globals.css` is imported in `layout.tsx` and contains the Tailwind import (`@import "tailwindcss"` for v4, or `@tailwind base; @tailwind components; @tailwind utilities;` for v3). Also verify `postcss.config.mjs` has the correct plugin. - **ESLint errors on fresh project** -- Run `npx next lint` to see errors. If rules are too strict, edit `.eslintrc.json` to extend only `next/core-web-vitals`. For flat config, check `eslint.config.mjs`. - **TypeScript `Cannot find module 'next/...'` in IDE** -- Run `npm install` to ensure all types are installed. Restart the TypeScript server in your editor (VS Code: Cmd+Shift+P > TypeScript: Restart TS Server). - **`next.config.mjs` vs `next.config.ts`** -- Next.js 15+ supports `.ts` config natively. Older versions use `.mjs`. Do not mix formats. Use whichever create-next-app generated. ### What NOT to Do - Do NOT run `create-next-app` inside an existing project directory with a `package.json` -- it will fail or create a nested mess. Always scaffold in a new directory. - Do NOT use `--no-eslint` to skip ESLint setup -- ESLint catches real bugs in Next.js (missing keys, invalid image usage, etc.). Keep it. - Do NOT change the import alias from `@/*` to something non-standard unless the user explicitly requests it -- nearly all Next.js ecosystem tools and tutorials assume `@/*`. - Do NOT delete `layout.tsx` -- it is required by the App Router. Every route segment needs a layout, and the root layout must include `` and `` tags. - Do NOT use Pages Router (`--no-app`) for new projects unless the user specifically needs it -- App Router is the default and recommended architecture since Next.js 13.4. - Do NOT skip `--typescript` for new projects -- TypeScript catches entire categories of bugs at build time. The performance cost is zero at runtime. - Do NOT manually install `next`, `react`, `react-dom` separately -- `create-next-app` handles dependency installation with correct version compatibility. - Do NOT commit `node_modules/` or `.next/` to git -- they are generated artifacts and must be in `.gitignore`. - Do NOT install global packages (`npm install -g next`) -- use `npx` for CLI tools to always get the latest version.

skills.title: Cut a release: version consistency, changelog truth, bare gates, tag, publish
skills.summary: Checklist-driven release flow that catches the usual silent failures: version strings that disagree across manifests, changelogs written from memory instead of the diff, gates that pass because of a pipe, and races with concurrent writers.
skills.declaration: I will follow the release checklist: sync with the remote, make every version string agree, verify the changelog against the actual diff since the last tag, run the verification suite bare (no piped gates), then tag, publish, and confirm CI is green.
skills.instructions:
## Cut a release

### 1. Sync and scope
- `git fetch` first, and again immediately before any push (another writer may be active).
- Establish what is being released: `git log <last-tag>..origin/<default-branch> --oneline`. The release contains exactly this — nothing you remember, everything you forgot.

### 2. Version consistency (grep, don't recall)
- Grep the OLD version string across the whole repo: manifests, lockfiles, server metadata, docs, embedded constants. Every hit either gets bumped or gets a reason.
- Lockfiles carry their own version fields — regenerate (`npm install --package-lock-only`) rather than hand-editing.
- If any component reports its version at runtime, make it read the manifest instead of a hardcoded string.

### 3. Changelog = the diff, not memory
- Write the release section from the commit list in step 1. Cross off every commit as it lands in a bullet; leftovers are either missing entries or deliberate omissions you can name.
- Set the section date to the ACTUAL release date. Update comparison links (`[Unreleased]`, the new tag).

### 4. Verification gates — bare, never piped
- Run each gate as a bare command and check its exit code: build, full test suite, smoke/demo, benchmarks if present.
- Never gate on `cmd | tail` / `cmd | grep` without pipefail — the pipe's exit code is the formatter's, not the gate's.
- Run at least one end-to-end check of the release artifact itself (the built dist / package), not just the source tree.

### 5. Tag and release
- Annotated tag on the exact verified commit. Extract release notes from the changelog section (one source of truth) rather than rewriting them.
- After pushing, confirm CI is green on the released commit before announcing.

### 6. Publish (registry)
- Auth first: `npm whoami` (or the registry equivalent). A publish-time 404/403 usually means a dead token, not a missing package — registries hide package existence from unauthorized callers.
- Publish, then verify from the outside: `npm view <pkg> version` must show the new version.

## Common Errors
- Version bumped in the main manifest but not the lockfile or secondary manifests (server metadata, docs badges) — grep the old string, always.
- Changelog describes what you INTENDED to ship; commits since the last tag tell what you actually shipped.
- A piped verification gate that passes while the underlying command fails.
- Tagging while a concurrent session/CI job is still pushing — fetch immediately before tag/push; on a rejected push merge, never force.
- Announcing on 'tests passed locally' before CI confirms the released commit.

## What NOT to do
- Do NOT move or reuse a tag that has been pushed; cut a new patch release instead.
- Do NOT publish to a registry before the tag's CI is green.
- Do NOT hand-edit generated lockfiles.
- Do NOT skip the outside-in verification (`npm view`, fresh install) after publishing.

skills.title: Delegate work to (weaker) subagents so the team beats a stronger soloist
skills.summary: The delegation discipline that lets a team of cheaper agents match a stronger single model: closed-form prompts, evidence-first verification, anti-correlated lenses, externalized state, and depth concentrated at the narrow waist.
skills.declaration: I will delegate with the closed-form template — every subagent gets a role, exact inputs, a definition of done, required evidence, known traps, and a single question — and I will verify claims by refutation, not agreement.
skills.instructions:
## Why this works
Generation is hard; verification is easy. A weaker model checking a narrow, falsifiable claim performs close to a stronger one. So convert open-ended work into (many cheap generations) + (mechanical verification) + (a few small judgments), and spend your strongest inference only at the narrow waist: decomposition and final judgment.

### 1. Never send an open-ended prompt
Every subagent prompt has six fields:
- **Role** — one sentence, adversarial roles stated as such ("you are a refuting reviewer").
- **Inputs** — exact paths/data, absolute, nothing to discover.
- **Definition of done** — the shape of the answer (schema, list length, severity scale).
- **Required evidence** — what proof must accompany every claim: file:line, command output, failing test. No evidence, no claim.
- **Known traps** — paste relevant scars (from kira_lookup / kira_premortem on the subtask keyword) so yesterday's judgment rides along with today's cheap executor.
- **The one question** — a single ask per agent. Two questions means two agents.

### 2. Verify by refutation, not agreement
- Generator and verifier are different agents. The verifier's instruction is "try to refute; when uncertain, refute" — asking "is this correct?" invites agreement bias.
- For important claims use 3+ independent verifiers and majority vote.

### 3. De-correlate the team
Same-model agents share blind spots; N identical reviewers are barely better than one. Force diversity structurally: different lenses (correctness / staleness / security / economy / wiring), different design philosophies (minimal / rebuild / pragmatic), different entry points into the material.

### 4. Externalize all state
Agents write full results to files; the orchestrator keeps only decisions and pointers. The team's shared brain lives on disk (task list, decision log, scars) — any individual agent can be stateless and replaceable.

### 5. Let the environment be the senior engineer
Prefer subtasks with ground truth: run the test, execute the command, measure. A weak agent that runs the code beats a strong one that reasons about it. Where ground truth is missing, first invest in making the work checkable (a failing test, a reproducible command), then delegate.

### 6. Wiring audit before declaring done
For any feature-shaped result, ask the closing questions: does every write path have a wired read path (proved end-to-end)? Does it run in the PRODUCTION configuration, not just in tests? Do the claimed numbers trace to real data?

## Common Errors
- Open-ended prompts ("review this repo") — coverage becomes untestable and results unmergeable.
- Leading verification ("confirm that X") instead of refutation.
- All reviewers share one lens → correlated misses reported with high confidence.
- Accepting claims without evidence artifacts; a plausible paragraph is not a finding.
- Two agents writing the same file or branch — races and lost work; give each a worktree/branch.
- The orchestrator pasting full agent outputs into its own context until it drowns; keep results in files.

## What NOT to do
- Do NOT skip the Required-evidence field even for 'trivial' subtasks — that is where silent failures hide.
- Do NOT resolve verifier disagreement by averaging; disagreement is signal — send a tie-breaker with both arguments.
- Do NOT let the strongest model do the cheap work; concentrate it where judgment compounds (decomposition, final merge, tie-breaks).

skills.title: Deploy a project to Cloudflare Pages
skills.summary: Complete Cloudflare Pages deployment flow — handles wrangler setup, auth, Next.js via @cloudflare/next-on-pages, static site deploys, env vars, secrets, custom domains, and preview deployments.
skills.declaration: Cloudflare Pages provides edge-first hosting with generous free tier. I will use the Wrangler CLI to deploy this project. If you prefer Vercel, Netlify, or a self-hosted alternative, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Deploy to Cloudflare Pages

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Verify `package.json` exists and has a `build` script.
- Determine the project type:
  - **Next.js**: Check for `next.config.js` or `next.config.mjs`. Will use `@cloudflare/next-on-pages`.
  - **Vite/React SPA**: Check for `vite.config.ts`. Output dir is typically `dist`.
  - **Static site**: No framework — deploy output directory directly.
- Run the build locally: `npm run build`. If this fails, DO NOT proceed — fix the build first.
- Verify the working tree is clean, or confirm with the user that they want to deploy uncommitted changes.

### 2. Wrangler CLI setup
- Check if Wrangler is installed: `npx wrangler --version`.
- If not available, install: `npm i -D wrangler`.
- Check auth status: `npx wrangler whoami`.
- If not logged in, run `npx wrangler login` and wait for the user to complete the browser OAuth flow.
- NEVER ask for or hardcode API tokens. Use `wrangler login` for interactive auth or `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` env var for CI.

### 3. Framework-specific setup

#### 3a. Next.js with @cloudflare/next-on-pages
- Install the adapter: `npm i -D @cloudflare/next-on-pages`.
- Add build script to `package.json`: `"pages:build": "npx @cloudflare/next-on-pages"`.
- Update `next.config.js`/`next.config.mjs`:
  - Set `runtime: 'edge'` on API routes and pages that need it, OR use the `export const runtime = 'edge'` directive in individual route files.
  - Ensure no Node.js-only APIs are used in edge routes (e.g., `fs`, `path`, `child_process`). If they are, refactor to use Web APIs or move to a non-edge route.
- Create or update `wrangler.toml` at project root:
  ```
  name = "<project-name>"
  compatibility_date = "2024-09-23"
  compatibility_flags = ["nodejs_compat"]
  pages_build_output_dir = ".vercel/output/static"
  ```
- Build: `npm run pages:build`.
- If build errors mention Node.js APIs not available, add `nodejs_compat` to compatibility_flags in `wrangler.toml` and retry.

#### 3b. Vite / React SPA / Static sites
- Identify the output directory (`dist` for Vite, `build` for CRA, `out` for static Next.js export).
- Create or update `wrangler.toml`:
  ```
  name = "<project-name>"
  compatibility_date = "2024-09-23"
  pages_build_output_dir = "dist"
  ```
- For SPAs with client-side routing, create a `public/_redirects` file:
  ```
  /* /index.html 200
  ```

### 4. Environment variables and secrets
- Scan for `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.production`, or `process.env` / `import.meta.env` references.
- For build-time env vars (e.g., `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`, `VITE_*`), set them in the Cloudflare dashboard or pass via CLI:
  `npx wrangler pages deploy <dir> --var KEY:value` (for non-sensitive values only).
- For runtime secrets (API keys, DB URLs), use: `npx wrangler pages secret put SECRET_NAME`.
  - This prompts interactively for the value — NEVER pass secrets as CLI arguments.
- To list existing secrets: `npx wrangler pages secret list --project-name=<name>`.
- NEVER commit `.env*` files. NEVER hardcode secrets in source or `wrangler.toml`.

### 5. Deploy
- **First-time project creation + deploy**:
  ```
  npx wrangler pages deploy <output-dir> --project-name=<project-name>
  ```
  If the project doesn't exist, Wrangler will create it automatically.
- **For Next.js** (after `pages:build`):
  ```
  npx wrangler pages deploy .vercel/output/static --project-name=<project-name>
  ```
- **Preview deploy** (default — no flags needed): `npx wrangler pages deploy <dir>`.
- **Production deploy**: `npx wrangler pages deploy <dir> --branch=main`.
  ALWAYS confirm with the user before deploying to production.
- Capture the deployment URL from the CLI output (format: `https://<hash>.<project>.pages.dev`).

### 6. Custom domains
- After first successful deploy, set up custom domain if requested:
  1. Go to Cloudflare dashboard > Pages > project > Custom domains, OR use:
     `npx wrangler pages project list` to confirm the project exists.
  2. Add custom domain via dashboard (CLI support is limited for domain management).
  3. If the domain is already on Cloudflare DNS, it auto-configures. If external, add the CNAME record:
     `CNAME <subdomain> <project>.pages.dev`
  4. SSL is automatic — no action needed.

### 7. Preview deployments on PRs
- Cloudflare Pages automatically creates preview deployments for every non-production branch.
- To enable GitHub integration for automatic PR previews:
  1. Connect repository via Cloudflare dashboard > Pages > project > Settings > Builds & deployments > Connect to Git.
  2. Set production branch to `main` (or `master`).
  3. Every PR push creates a preview URL: `https://<branch>.<project>.pages.dev`.
- Alternatively, deploy manually from any branch:
  `npx wrangler pages deploy <dir> --branch=<branch-name>`.

### 8. Bindings (D1, KV, R2, etc.)
- If the project uses Cloudflare D1, KV, R2, or other bindings, configure in `wrangler.toml`:
  ```
  [[d1_databases]]
  binding = "DB"
  database_name = "my-db"
  database_id = "<uuid>"

  [[kv_namespaces]]
  binding = "KV"
  id = "<namespace-id>"
  ```
- Bindings are accessed via the `platform.env` object in Next.js or the `env` parameter in Workers.
- Missing bindings at runtime produce `ReferenceError: <BINDING> is not defined` — always verify bindings are configured.

### 9. Verify
- Fetch the deployment URL and confirm HTTP 200: `curl -sL -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' <url>`.
- If the deploy failed, check build logs in the Cloudflare dashboard or: `npx wrangler pages deployment list --project-name=<name>`.
- For Next.js, test edge routes specifically — they may fail even if static pages work.
- Report the live URL to the user.

### Differences from Vercel (important)
- **Edge runtime required**: Cloudflare Pages runs on Workers (V8 isolates), not Node.js. Server-side code must be edge-compatible.
- **No Node.js APIs by default**: `fs`, `path`, `crypto` (Node version), `Buffer` etc. are unavailable unless `nodejs_compat` flag is set.
- **No ISR/SSG revalidation**: Cloudflare Pages does not support Next.js ISR natively. Use `export const revalidate = 0` (SSR) or full static export.
- **No `next/image` optimization out of the box**: Use Cloudflare Images or a custom loader.
- **Middleware**: Supported but must be edge-compatible. No `response.rewrite()` with full URLs.
- **Monorepo**: Set the root directory in project settings if the app is in a subdirectory.

### Common errors & fixes
- **'Could not resolve Node.js built-in module'** → Add `nodejs_compat` to `compatibility_flags` in `wrangler.toml`.
- **'Error: Failed to produce a Cloudflare Pages build'** (Next.js) → Ensure `@cloudflare/next-on-pages` is installed and the build command is `npx @cloudflare/next-on-pages`.
- **'Your Worker exceeded the size limit'** → Reduce bundle size. Check for large dependencies. Use dynamic imports. Worker size limit is 25 MB (free) / 10 MB compressed.
- **'Binding not found'** → Verify `wrangler.toml` has the correct binding configuration. Bindings must match between local dev and deployed.
- **'Error: No project with name X'** → The project name in `--project-name` must match exactly. Run `npx wrangler pages project list` to check.
- **404 on client-side routes (SPA)** → Add `_redirects` file with `/* /index.html 200`.
- **Build works locally but fails on Cloudflare** → Check Node.js version compatibility. Set `NODE_VERSION` env var in build settings.
- **'compatibility_date is too old'** → Update to a recent date (within last 6 months).

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT use Node.js-only APIs (`fs`, `path`, `child_process`, `net`) in edge/worker code without enabling `nodejs_compat`.
- Do NOT deploy without testing edge runtime compatibility first — run `npx wrangler pages dev` locally.
- Do NOT hardcode API tokens or `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` in code or config files.
- Do NOT run production deploys (`--branch=main`) without explicit user confirmation.
- Do NOT assume Vercel-specific features (ISR, `next/image` optimization, serverless functions) work identically on Cloudflare.
- Do NOT skip `wrangler.toml` creation — deploying without it leads to misconfigured projects.
- Do NOT use `wrangler publish` — that's for Workers, not Pages. Use `wrangler pages deploy`.

skills.title: Deploy a Next.js project to Vercel
skills.summary: Complete Vercel deployment flow for Next.js apps — handles auth, build verification, env vars, first-time linking, and production confirmation.
skills.declaration: Vercel provides a first-class, officially-supported deployment path for Next.js. I will use the Vercel CLI to deploy this project. If you prefer Cloudflare Pages, Netlify, or a self-hosted alternative, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Deploy Next.js to Vercel

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Verify `package.json` exists and has a `build` script (typically `next build`).
- Run the build locally first: `npm run build`. If this fails, DO NOT proceed — fix the build first.
- Verify the working tree is clean, or confirm with the user that they want to deploy uncommitted changes.

### 2. Vercel CLI setup
- Check if the Vercel CLI is installed: `vercel --version`.
- If not installed, install globally: `npm i -g vercel` (warn user if global install needs sudo).
- Check auth status: `vercel whoami`.
- If not logged in, run `vercel login` and wait for the user to complete the browser flow.

### 3. Project linking (first-time only)
- If no `.vercel/project.json` exists, run `vercel link`.
- Ask the user: existing project or new? Which scope (personal or team)?
- Confirm the project name before proceeding.

### 4. Environment variables
- Look at `.env.local`, `.env.production`, or references to `process.env` in the code.
- If env vars are used, ensure they exist on Vercel: `vercel env ls`.
- Missing critical env vars → STOP and ask the user to add them before deploying. Never commit `.env*` files.

### 5. Deploy
- Preview deploy: `vercel`.
- Production deploy: `vercel --prod`. ALWAYS confirm with the user before running `--prod`.
- Capture the deployment URL from the CLI output.

### 6. Verify
- Fetch the deployment URL and confirm HTTP 200.
- If the deploy failed, surface the build log: `vercel logs <url>`.
- Report the live URL to the user.

### Common errors & fixes
- **'No framework detected'** → Add `"framework": "nextjs"` to `vercel.json`, or re-run `vercel link`.
- **Build succeeds locally, fails on Vercel** → Likely Node version mismatch. Set `engines.node` in `package.json`.
- **'Missing environment variable'** → Run `vercel env add <NAME>` for each missing var, then redeploy.
- **Auth loop** → `vercel logout && vercel login` to reset the session.
- **Edge function size limit** → Check for large imports in middleware; move heavy work to serverless functions.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT run `vercel --prod` without explicit user confirmation.
- Do NOT commit the `.vercel/` directory beyond `project.json` — it may contain tokens.
- Do NOT hard-code env var values into source 'just to get the deploy working'.
- Do NOT skip the local build check in step 1 to save time — failing on Vercel wastes more time.

skills.title: List an MCP server in the official MCP Registry (OIDC from CI, no device flow)
skills.summary: Get an MCP server into registry.modelcontextprotocol.io — which PulseMCP, Smithery and other aggregators auto-ingest — as a repeatable CI job instead of a one-off manual ritual. Verified on a real listing, including the schema traps.
skills.declaration: I will publish this MCP server to the official registry via a CI job using GitHub Actions OIDC (io.github.<owner> namespace), with the server.json validated against the live schema, and verify the listing from the outside afterwards.
skills.instructions:
## Publish to the MCP Server Registry

### 0. Know the landscape
The old `modelcontextprotocol/servers` repo no longer accepts third-party PRs. The canonical path is the **MCP Server Registry** (`registry.modelcontextprotocol.io`), and aggregators (PulseMCP, Smithery) ingest it automatically — one listing propagates.

### 1. Prepare the metadata
- `package.json`: add `"mcpName": "io.github.<owner>/<name>"`.
- `server.json` at the repo root against the registry schema: identity, packages (npm descriptor with the CURRENT version), tools surface.
- **description must be <= 100 characters** — the live schema rejects longer with a 422 at publish time, not at authoring time.
- Keep `server.json` version fields in lockstep with `package.json` on every release (script it or grep the old version repo-wide).

### 2. Publish from CI with OIDC (the repeatable way)
A workflow with `permissions: { id-token: write }` can authenticate as the repo itself — `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` proves the `io.github.<owner>/*` namespace with no token and no device flow. Steps:
- Download a PINNED `mcp-publisher` release binary and verify its sha256 (`echo "$SHA256  file.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c -`) before executing — this job holds a credential.
- `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc && ./mcp-publisher publish`.
- Verify from the outside: `curl -fsS 'https://registry.modelcontextprotocol.io/v0.1/servers?search=<name>'` must contain your id.
- Trigger on release tags (with a `git merge-base --is-ancestor` gate) plus `workflow_dispatch` for the first listing.

### 3. If you must publish from a laptop instead
`mcp-publisher login github` runs a browser device flow (needs the human). Anonymous auth (`login none`) no longer exists on the live registry — don't plan around it.

## Common Errors
- `description` longer than 100 chars → 422 `expected length <= 100` only at publish time; the file authors fine locally.
- Expecting `mcp-publisher publish --dry-run` to work unauthenticated — the anonymous token endpoint is gone (404); validation effectively happens on the authenticated publish.
- Submitting a PR to `modelcontextprotocol/servers` — closed to third parties; you'll wait forever.
- `server.json` version drifting from the released npm version because only `package.json` was bumped.
- Downloading `mcp-publisher` from `releases/latest` in CI — unpinned binary in a credential-holding job; pin the version AND the checksum.

## What NOT to do
- Do NOT run the interactive device-flow login in CI — use `github-oidc`, that's what it exists for.
- Do NOT list a server whose npm package isn't actually installable at the declared version — aggregators will surface a broken install as the first impression.
- Do NOT skip the outside-in search check after publishing; a green CI job is not a listing.

skills.title: Publish to npm from GitHub Actions with Trusted Publishing (OIDC, no tokens)
skills.summary: Tag-push releases with zero long-lived credentials: OIDC proves the repo's identity, npm publishes with a provenance attestation. Every step here was verified on a real release, including the traps.
skills.declaration: I will set up tokenless npm publishing: a tag-triggered workflow that verifies the tag is on the default branch, publishes with OIDC + provenance, and confirms the version from the registry afterwards. One manual step remains for the package owner: registering the Trusted Publisher on npmjs.com.
skills.instructions:
## npm Trusted Publishing from GitHub Actions

### 1. Workflow (tag-triggered)
Create `.github/workflows/release.yml` with two jobs:
- **verify** (no credentials): checkout with `fetch-depth: 0`, then gate hard — `git fetch origin main && git merge-base --is-ancestor "$GITHUB_SHA" origin/main` (never publish a tag that isn't on the reviewed branch), build, full tests, and check the tag equals `package.json` version (`TAG_V="${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}"`).
- **publish** (credential-holding): `permissions: { contents: read, id-token: write }` — the id-token IS the whole credential. Steps: setup-node with `registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org`, install a pinned npm CLI (`npm install -g npm@11.5.1` — trusted publishing needs >= 11.5.1; never `@latest` in a credential job), build, then `npm publish --provenance --access public`.
- Use Node 22+ in the publish job (documented floor for trusted publishing).

### 2. One-time registration on npmjs.com (package owner, in a browser)
Package page → **Settings → Trusted Publisher** → GitHub Actions, then fill EXACTLY (case-sensitive):
- Organization or user: the repo owner
- Repository: the repo name
- Workflow filename: `release.yml` (filename only, with extension)
- Environment name: leave EMPTY unless the workflow really uses a GitHub Environment
- Allowed actions: select `npm publish` (skip `npm stage publish` unless you stage)

The form is NOT validated at save time — typos only surface as a failed publish later.

### 3. Release
`git tag -a vX.Y.Z <sha-on-main> && git push origin vX.Y.Z`. The workflow verifies, publishes with a provenance attestation (visible on the npm page and in the sigstore transparency log), and should end by checking from the outside: `npm view <pkg> version` equals the tag.

### 4. Read failures correctly
`npm publish` returning **404 'could not be found or you do not have permission'** is almost always an AUTH failure, not a missing package — npm hides package existence from unauthorized callers. If OIDC login succeeded and provenance signed but the final PUT 404s, the Trusted Publisher registration is missing or one field mismatches.

## Common Errors
- Reading publish-time 404 as a registry/package-name problem and debugging the wrong layer (it means: registration absent or field mismatch, or a dead token in the legacy flow).
- Filling the Environment field on npmjs.com when the workflow declares no environment — the OIDC claims then never match.
- npm CLI too old on the runner (bundled npm of Node 20 is < 11.5.1) — install the pinned newer CLI explicitly.
- Publishing a tag that isn't an ancestor of the default branch — enforce with `git merge-base --is-ancestor` in CI, don't rely on habit.
- `npm install -g npm@latest` inside the credential-holding job — unpinned supply chain; pin the exact version.

## What NOT to do
- Do NOT keep a long-lived npm token in repo secrets once trusted publishing works — delete the old token; the whole point is that no stealable credential exists.
- Do NOT move or reuse a pushed tag to re-trigger a release; cut a new patch version.
- Do NOT announce the release before the workflow's outside-in check (`npm view`) confirms the registry serves the new version.

skills.title: Set up Auth.js v5 in a Next.js App Router project
skills.summary: Complete Auth.js v5 setup — install, route handler, middleware, providers (Google/GitHub/Credentials), session access in server & client components, and database adapter.
skills.declaration: I will set up Auth.js v5 (the successor to NextAuth.js v4) for authentication. Auth.js v5 is designed for the App Router and supports Edge Runtime. If you prefer Clerk, Lucia, or another auth solution, say so before I begin.

skills.title: Set up Clerk authentication with Next.js App Router
skills.summary: Complete Clerk auth integration for Next.js App Router — ClerkProvider, middleware, sign-in/sign-up pages, route protection, and server/client auth hooks.
skills.declaration: I will set up Clerk authentication using @clerk/nextjs for the Next.js App Router. This covers middleware, provider setup, auth pages, and route protection. If you need a different auth provider (NextAuth, Supabase Auth, Auth0), say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set up Clerk Authentication with Next.js App Router ### 1. Pre-flight checks - Verify this is a Next.js 13+ project using the App Router (check for `app/` directory and `next.config.*`). - Check if `src/` directory exists — this determines where `middleware.ts` goes. - Check if any existing auth solution is installed (`next-auth`, `@supabase/auth-helpers-nextjs`, etc.). If so, STOP and ask the user how to handle the conflict. - Verify `package.json` exists. ### 2. Install @clerk/nextjs - Run: `npm install @clerk/nextjs@latest` - Verify installation succeeded by checking `node_modules/@clerk/nextjs/package.json` exists. ### 3. Environment variables - Check if `.env.local` exists. If not, create it. - Add the following keys (DO NOT fill in values — instruct the user to get them from https://dashboard.clerk.com): ``` NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY= CLERK_SECRET_KEY= NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_IN_URL=/sign-in NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_UP_URL=/sign-up NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_AFTER_SIGN_IN_URL=/ NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_AFTER_SIGN_UP_URL=/ ``` - **CRITICAL**: `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` must NEVER have the `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix. It is server-only. Exposing it to the client is a security vulnerability. - Tell the user to paste their keys from the Clerk Dashboard before proceeding. ### 4. ClerkProvider in root layout - Open `app/layout.tsx` (or `src/app/layout.tsx` if using `src/`). - Import `ClerkProvider` from `@clerk/nextjs`. - Wrap the entire `{children}` in ``. It MUST be inside `` and `` tags. - Example: ```tsx import { ClerkProvider } from '@clerk/nextjs' export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) { return ( {children} ) } ``` - **NOTE**: `ClerkProvider` is a Server Component wrapper. Do NOT add `'use client'` to the root layout just for this. ### 5. Middleware (CRITICAL — most common source of errors) - **File location rules**: - If the project uses `src/` directory: create `src/middleware.ts` - If NO `src/` directory: create `middleware.ts` at the project root (same level as `package.json`) - **NEVER** put middleware inside `app/` directory — Next.js will silently ignore it - **NEVER** put middleware inside `pages/` directory - Write the middleware file: ```typescript import { clerkMiddleware, createRouteMatcher } from '@clerk/nextjs/server' const isPublicRoute = createRouteMatcher([ '/sign-in(.*)', '/sign-up(.*)', '/', '/api/webhooks(.*)', ]) export default clerkMiddleware(async (auth, request) => { if (!isPublicRoute(request)) { await auth.protect() } }) export const config = { matcher: [ // Skip Next.js internals and all static files, unless found in search params '/((?!_next|[^?]*\\.(?:html?|css|js(?!on)|jpe?g|webp|png|gif|svg|ttf|woff2?|ico|csv|docx?|xlsx?|zip|webmanifest)).*)', // Always run for API routes '/(api|trpc)(.*)', ], } ``` - **IMPORTANT**: The `matcher` config is what tells Next.js which routes to run middleware on. Without it, middleware runs on EVERY request including static files, which destroys performance. - **IMPORTANT**: `createRouteMatcher` uses path patterns, NOT glob patterns. Parentheses `(.*)` are regex groups. - After creating the file, verify the path is correct relative to the project root. ### 6. Sign-in and sign-up pages - Create catch-all route for sign-in: `app/sign-in/[[...sign-in]]/page.tsx` ```tsx import { SignIn } from '@clerk/nextjs' export default function SignInPage() { return (
) } ``` - Create catch-all route for sign-up: `app/sign-up/[[...sign-up]]/page.tsx` ```tsx import { SignUp } from '@clerk/nextjs' export default function SignUpPage() { return (
) } ``` - The `[[...sign-in]]` catch-all is required because Clerk uses multi-step flows with sub-routes. - These paths MUST match `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_IN_URL` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLERK_SIGN_UP_URL` env vars. ### 7. Using auth in server components - In Server Components and Server Actions, use `auth()` from `@clerk/nextjs/server`: ```tsx import { auth } from '@clerk/nextjs/server' export default async function ProtectedPage() { const { userId } = await auth() if (!userId) { // redirect or show unauthorized } return
Protected content for user {userId}
} ``` - `auth()` is async in Clerk v5+. Always `await` it. - For getting the full user object server-side: use `currentUser()` from `@clerk/nextjs/server`. ### 8. Using auth in client components - In Client Components (`'use client'`), use hooks: ```tsx 'use client' import { useAuth, useUser } from '@clerk/nextjs' export function UserProfile() { const { isLoaded, userId, isSignedIn } = useAuth() const { user } = useUser() if (!isLoaded) return
Loading...
if (!isSignedIn) return
Not signed in
return
Hello, {user?.firstName}
} ``` - These hooks ONLY work inside `ClerkProvider`. If you get "useAuth can only be used within ClerkProvider", the provider is missing or not wrapping the component tree. - `useAuth()` for auth state (lightweight). `useUser()` for full user data (heavier). - **UserButton** component for a drop-in profile menu: `import { UserButton } from '@clerk/nextjs'` ### 9. Protecting API routes - For Route Handlers (`app/api/*/route.ts`): ```typescript import { auth } from '@clerk/nextjs/server' import { NextResponse } from 'next/server' export async function GET() { const { userId } = await auth() if (!userId) { return NextResponse.json({ error: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 }) } // ... handle authenticated request } ``` - The middleware already protects routes not in `isPublicRoute`, but explicit checks in API routes provide defense-in-depth. ### 10. Verify the setup - Start the dev server: `npm run dev` - Visit `http://localhost:3000` — should load without errors. - Visit a protected route — should redirect to `/sign-in`. - Check the terminal for middleware-related errors. - If middleware is not firing, verify: (a) file location is correct, (b) `matcher` config is present, (c) the dev server was restarted after creating `middleware.ts`. ### Common errors & fixes - **Middleware not firing** → Wrong file location. Must be `src/middleware.ts` (with src/) or `./middleware.ts` (without src/). NEVER inside `app/`. - **"ClerkProvider is required"** → Root layout is not wrapping children with ``. - **Hydration errors** → Likely mixing server and client auth. `auth()` is server-only, `useAuth()`/`useUser()` are client-only. Never cross the boundary. - **"CLERK_SECRET_KEY is missing"** → `.env.local` not loaded. Restart dev server. Ensure no typos. - **Sign-in page 404** → The catch-all route structure is wrong. Must be `app/sign-in/[[...sign-in]]/page.tsx` (double brackets). - **Infinite redirect loop** → Sign-in/sign-up routes are not in `isPublicRoute`. Add them to `createRouteMatcher`. - **API route returns 401 unexpectedly** → The route path is not in the middleware `matcher`, or it's not in `isPublicRoute` when it should be. - **"Cannot find module @clerk/nextjs/server"** → Old version of @clerk/nextjs. Run `npm install @clerk/nextjs@latest`. ### What NOT to do - Do NOT put `middleware.ts` inside the `app/` directory — Next.js ignores it there. - Do NOT add `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix to `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` — this exposes the secret to the browser. - Do NOT use `useAuth()` or `useUser()` in Server Components — they are client-only hooks. - Do NOT use `auth()` in Client Components — it is server-only. - Do NOT skip the `matcher` config in middleware — it will run on every request including static assets. - Do NOT forget to add sign-in/sign-up routes to `isPublicRoute` — this causes infinite redirect loops. - Do NOT use the deprecated `authMiddleware` — use `clerkMiddleware` (Clerk v5+). - Do NOT hardcode Clerk keys in source code — always use environment variables.

skills.title: Set up Docker for Node.js and Next.js projects
skills.summary: Production-grade Docker setup — multi-stage Dockerfile, .dockerignore, docker-compose for dev, health checks, and Next.js standalone output.
skills.declaration: I will create a production-grade Docker setup with multi-stage builds, proper layer caching, a non-root user, and minimal image size. For Next.js projects, I will use standalone output mode. If you need a different base image or orchestration tool (e.g., Kubernetes), say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set up Docker for Node.js / Next.js

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Verify `package.json` exists and has `start` and `build` scripts.
- Detect the framework: Next.js (`next` in deps) vs plain Node.js.
- Check if Docker is installed: `docker --version`. If not, tell the user to install Docker Desktop or Docker Engine first.
- Check if a `Dockerfile` already exists — if so, ask before overwriting.
- Check for existing `.dockerignore`.
- For Next.js: check `next.config.js` or `next.config.mjs` for the `output` setting.

### 2. Create .dockerignore
- Create `.dockerignore` in the project root with:
```
node_modules
.next
.git
.gitignore
*.md
.env
.env.*
.cache
.vscode
.idea
coverage
.nyc_output
dist
build
Dockerfile
docker-compose*.yml
.dockerignore
```
- CRITICAL: Without `.dockerignore`, `COPY . .` sends `node_modules` (often 500MB+) to the Docker daemon, causing massive images and slow builds.

### 3. Configure Next.js standalone output (Next.js only)
- If the project uses Next.js, ensure `next.config.mjs` (or `.js`) has standalone output:
```js
const nextConfig = {
  output: "standalone",
};
export default nextConfig;
```
- Standalone mode produces a self-contained `server.js` that includes only the dependencies needed at runtime, reducing image size from ~1GB to ~100-150MB.
- After adding this, run `npm run build` locally to verify it generates `.next/standalone/`.

### 4. Create multi-stage Dockerfile

#### For Next.js projects:
```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Install dependencies
FROM node:22-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --ignore-scripts

# Stage 2: Build the application
FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

# Stage 3: Production image
FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs

COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static

USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=15s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1

CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

#### For plain Node.js projects:
```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Install dependencies
FROM node:22-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --ignore-scripts

# Stage 2: Build (if TypeScript or build step exists)
FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 3: Production image
FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production

RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 appgroup
RUN adduser --system --uid 1001 appuser

COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./

USER appuser
EXPOSE 3000

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=10s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
```
- Adjust the `COPY --from=builder` paths to match the project's actual build output directory (`dist`, `build`, `out`, etc.).
- If the project has no build step (plain JS, no TypeScript), skip the builder stage and copy source directly.

### 5. Key Dockerfile principles
- **Layer caching**: ALWAYS `COPY package.json package-lock.json ./` and `RUN npm ci` BEFORE `COPY . .`. This ensures `npm ci` is cached unless dependencies change.
- **npm ci over npm install**: `npm ci` is deterministic, faster, and respects `package-lock.json` exactly.
- **Alpine base**: `node:22-alpine` is ~50MB vs `node:22` at ~350MB. Use Alpine unless native modules require glibc.
- **Non-root user**: ALWAYS create and switch to a non-root user before `CMD`.
- **EXPOSE is documentation**: It does not publish ports. Users still need `-p 3000:3000`.
- **--ignore-scripts**: Prevents arbitrary scripts from running during `npm ci` in the build context.

### 6. Create docker-compose.yml for local development
```yaml
services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=development
    env_file:
      - .env
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - /app/node_modules
    restart: unless-stopped
```
- The `volumes` section mounts source code for hot-reload.
- `/app/node_modules` is an anonymous volume that prevents the host's `node_modules` from overriding the container's.
- `env_file` loads `.env` into the container. NEVER bake secrets into the image.

### 7. Build and test
- Build the image: `docker build -t <project-name> .`
- Check image size: `docker images <project-name>`. Target sizes:
  - Next.js standalone: 100-200MB
  - Plain Node.js: 80-150MB
  - If over 500MB, something is wrong (usually missing `.dockerignore` or not using multi-stage).
- Run the container: `docker run -p 3000:3000 --env-file .env <project-name>`
- Verify the app responds: `curl http://localhost:3000`
- Check health: `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' <container-id>`
- For docker-compose: `docker compose up --build`

### 8. Production considerations
- Pin the exact Node.js version in production: `node:22.14.0-alpine` instead of `node:22-alpine`.
- Use `--platform=linux/amd64` in the build if deploying to x86 servers from an ARM Mac:
```bash
docker build --platform=linux/amd64 -t <project-name> .
```
- Set memory limits in docker-compose for production:
```yaml
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 512M
```
- Use `.env.production` or secrets management — never bake env vars into the image with `ENV`.

### Common errors & fixes
- **Image is 1GB+** → Missing `.dockerignore` (node_modules copied in) or not using multi-stage builds. Add `.dockerignore` and use the multi-stage Dockerfile above.
- **`npm ci` runs on every build even without dependency changes** → `COPY . .` is before `COPY package*.json` + `npm ci`. Fix the order: copy `package.json` and `package-lock.json` first, run `npm ci`, THEN `COPY . .`.
- **`EACCES: permission denied`** → Running as root wrote files that the non-root user cannot read. Ensure `--chown` is used on `COPY` commands or `chown` the directory before switching to the non-root user.
- **Next.js standalone missing static files** → You must separately copy `public/` and `.next/static` — standalone mode does not include them.
- **Container starts but app is unreachable** → The app is listening on `127.0.0.1` instead of `0.0.0.0`. Set `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` or configure the framework to bind to all interfaces.
- **`Cannot find module` errors in production image** → Dependencies were not copied correctly. Ensure `node_modules` (or standalone output) is copied from the correct build stage.
- **Health check failing** → Container may not have `curl`. Use `wget` (included in Alpine) or install `curl` in the Dockerfile.
- **Build fails with native modules on Alpine** → Some packages need `python3`, `make`, `g++`. Add: `RUN apk add --no-cache python3 make g++` in the deps stage.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT run the container as root — always create and switch to a non-root user.
- Do NOT copy `node_modules` from the host into the image — always run `npm ci` inside the container.
- Do NOT use `:latest` tag for base images in production — pin a specific version.
- Do NOT skip `.dockerignore` — it is not optional. Without it, builds are slow and images are bloated.
- Do NOT use `COPY . .` before `npm ci` — this busts the layer cache on every source code change.
- Do NOT store secrets or env vars in the Dockerfile with `ENV` — use `env_file`, `--env-file`, or a secrets manager.
- Do NOT use `npm start` in production if the project has a standalone build — use `node server.js` directly.
- Do NOT install `devDependencies` in the production stage — `npm ci` with `NODE_ENV=production` or `npm ci --omit=dev` skips them.

skills.title: Set up Drizzle ORM in a Next.js project
skills.summary: Complete Drizzle ORM setup — install, config, schema, migrations, studio, and connection pooling for Neon/Supabase/Turso.
skills.declaration: I will set up Drizzle ORM as the database layer. Drizzle is a lightweight, type-safe ORM with zero dependencies. If you prefer Prisma instead, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set up Drizzle ORM in a Next.js project

### 1. Detect database provider
- Ask the user which database they are using (or detect from existing `.env*` files):
  - **Neon** (Postgres, serverless) — most common for Vercel deployments
  - **Supabase** (Postgres)
  - **PlanetScale** (MySQL)
  - **Turso** (SQLite/libSQL)
  - **Local Postgres** (Docker or native)
- If `DATABASE_URL` already exists in `.env` or `.env.local`, infer the provider from the connection string prefix.

### 2. Install packages
- Core (always): `npm install drizzle-orm` and `npm install -D drizzle-kit`
- Per provider:
  - **Neon**: `npm install @neondatabase/serverless` (uses HTTP driver, no TCP needed on edge)
  - **Supabase**: `npm install postgres` (use `postgres` package, NOT `pg`)
  - **PlanetScale**: `npm install @planetscale/database`
  - **Turso**: `npm install @libsql/client`
  - **Local Postgres**: `npm install postgres` (or `pg` if they prefer node-postgres)
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT install `pg` for serverless providers — it requires a TCP connection and breaks on edge runtimes.

### 3. Environment variables
- Ensure `DATABASE_URL` is set in `.env.local` (NOT `.env` — Next.js gitignores `.env.local` by default).
- For Neon pooled connections, the URL format is: `postgresql://user:pass@ep-xxx.region.aws.neon.tech/dbname?sslmode=require`
- For Turso: `TURSO_DATABASE_URL` and `TURSO_AUTH_TOKEN`.
- NEVER hard-code connection strings in source files.

### 4. Create database connection file
- Create `src/db/index.ts` (or `lib/db.ts` — match the project's existing convention).
- Content depends on provider:

**Neon example:**
```ts
import { neon } from '@neondatabase/serverless';
import { drizzle } from 'drizzle-orm/neon-http';
import * as schema from './schema';

const sql = neon(process.env.DATABASE_URL!);
export const db = drizzle(sql, { schema });
```

**Postgres (node) example:**
```ts
import { drizzle } from 'drizzle-orm/postgres-js';
import postgres from 'postgres';
import * as schema from './schema';

const client = postgres(process.env.DATABASE_URL!);
export const db = drizzle(client, { schema });
```

**Turso example:**
```ts
import { drizzle } from 'drizzle-orm/libsql';
import { createClient } from '@libsql/client';
import * as schema from './schema';

const client = createClient({
  url: process.env.TURSO_DATABASE_URL!,
  authToken: process.env.TURSO_AUTH_TOKEN!,
});
export const db = drizzle(client, { schema });
```

- IMPORTANT: Always pass `{ schema }` to `drizzle()` to enable the relational query API.
- IMPORTANT: For serverless environments, do NOT create a persistent connection pool. Neon HTTP driver and Turso HTTP are stateless by design.

### 5. Create schema file
- Create `src/db/schema.ts`.
- Use the correct column builders for the dialect:
  - Postgres: import from `drizzle-orm/pg-core`
  - MySQL: import from `drizzle-orm/mysql-core`
  - SQLite: import from `drizzle-orm/sqlite-core`
- Create a minimal starter table so migration generation works:
```ts
import { pgTable, text, timestamp, uuid } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';

export const users = pgTable('users', {
  id: uuid('id').defaultRandom().primaryKey(),
  name: text('name').notNull(),
  email: text('email').notNull().unique(),
  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow().notNull(),
});
```
- Export all tables from this file. The `db/index.ts` imports `* as schema` from here.

### 6. Create drizzle.config.ts
- Create `drizzle.config.ts` at project root:
```ts
import { defineConfig } from 'drizzle-kit';

export default defineConfig({
  schema: './src/db/schema.ts',
  out: './drizzle',
  dialect: 'postgresql', // or 'mysql' or 'sqlite'
  dbCredentials: {
    url: process.env.DATABASE_URL!,
  },
});
```
- IMPORTANT: `dialect` replaced the old `driver` field in drizzle-kit 0.21+. Do NOT use `driver`.
- IMPORTANT: The `out` directory (`./drizzle`) stores migration SQL files. This SHOULD be committed to git.

### 7. Add npm scripts
- Add to `package.json` scripts:
```json
"db:generate": "drizzle-kit generate",
"db:migrate": "drizzle-kit migrate",
"db:push": "drizzle-kit push",
"db:studio": "drizzle-kit studio"
```
- `db:push` is for rapid prototyping (applies schema directly, no migration files).
- `db:generate` + `db:migrate` is for production workflows (creates SQL migration files, then applies them).

### 8. Generate and run initial migration
- Run `npm run db:generate` — this creates a migration SQL file in `./drizzle/`.
- Run `npm run db:migrate` — this applies the migration to the database.
- Verify: check that the table exists by running `npm run db:studio` and inspecting in the browser.

### 9. Verify the setup works
- Create a quick test in a Next.js API route or server component:
```ts
import { db } from '@/db';
import { users } from '@/db/schema';

const allUsers = await db.select().from(users);
console.log(allUsers);
```
- Run `npm run dev` and hit the route. Confirm no errors.

### 10. TypeScript path alias
- Ensure `tsconfig.json` has a path alias that resolves `@/db` correctly:
```json
"paths": {
  "@/*": ["./src/*"]
}
```
- If the project uses `app/` at root (no `src/`), adjust the schema path in `drizzle.config.ts` and the import alias accordingly.

### Common errors & fixes
- **`Cannot find module 'drizzle-orm/neon-http'`** — You installed the wrong driver package. Ensure `@neondatabase/serverless` is installed, not `pg`.
- **`NeonDbError: connection is insecure`** — Add `?sslmode=require` to your DATABASE_URL.
- **`TypeError: sql is not a function`** — You imported `neon` wrong. It should be `const sql = neon(url)`, not `new Neon(url)`.
- **`relation "xxx" does not exist`** — You forgot to run `db:migrate` or `db:push` after adding a new table.
- **`drizzle-kit: Unknown dialect`** — You are on an old drizzle-kit version that uses `driver` instead of `dialect`. Update drizzle-kit to 0.21+ or use the `driver` field.
- **`Module not found: Can't resolve 'pg'`** — You used `drizzle-orm/node-postgres` but installed `postgres` (or vice versa). Match the import to the installed package.
- **Edge runtime error: `pg` uses Node.js TCP** — Switch to `@neondatabase/serverless` with `drizzle-orm/neon-http` for edge compatibility.
- **`prepared statement already exists`** — In serverless, each invocation can reuse a connection. Use `postgres({ prepare: false })` with the `postgres` package.
- **Migration drift** — Never manually edit files in `./drizzle/`. If you need to fix a migration, use `db:generate` to create a new one.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT use `pg` (node-postgres) with Neon serverless or Vercel Edge — it requires TCP and will fail.
- Do NOT put `DATABASE_URL` in `.env` if using Next.js — use `.env.local` so it is gitignored.
- Do NOT skip passing `{ schema }` to `drizzle()` — without it, the relational query builder (`db.query.users.findMany()`) will not work.
- Do NOT manually edit migration SQL files in `./drizzle/` — always use `drizzle-kit generate`.
- Do NOT use both `db:push` and `db:migrate` on the same database — pick one workflow. Push for prototyping, migrate for production.
- Do NOT import from `drizzle-orm/pg-core` when using MySQL or SQLite — column types are dialect-specific.
- Do NOT create a global connection pool in serverless environments — each request should use a fresh lightweight connection.
- Do NOT forget to add `./drizzle/` directory to version control — migration files must be tracked.

skills.title: Set up ESLint v9+ with flat config (eslint.config.mjs)
skills.summary: Complete ESLint v9+ flat config setup — TypeScript, React, Next.js compatibility, Prettier integration, and migration from legacy .eslintrc.
skills.declaration: I will set up ESLint v9+ using the flat config format (eslint.config.mjs). This replaces the legacy .eslintrc.* files. If you need ESLint v8 or a legacy config for compatibility reasons, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set up ESLint v9+ Flat Config

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Check the current ESLint version if installed: `npx eslint --version`.
- Check for existing legacy config files: `.eslintrc`, `.eslintrc.js`, `.eslintrc.json`, `.eslintrc.yml`, `.eslintrc.cjs`.
- If legacy config files exist, they MUST be removed or renamed before proceeding — ESLint v9 ignores them when `eslint.config.mjs` exists, but their presence causes confusion.
- Check `package.json` for existing ESLint-related dependencies to understand the current setup.
- Determine the project type: plain JS, TypeScript, React, Next.js, Node.js, or Vite. This determines which plugins to install.

### 2. Remove legacy config (if migrating)
- If `.eslintrc.*` files exist:
  - Read the existing config to understand current rules and plugins.
  - Note down any custom rules the user has configured.
  - Delete ALL legacy config files: `.eslintrc`, `.eslintrc.js`, `.eslintrc.json`, `.eslintrc.yml`, `.eslintrc.cjs`.
  - Also delete `.eslintignore` — flat config uses `ignores` array instead.
- If `eslint.config.js` exists (not `.mjs`), consider renaming to `.mjs` for ESM import support. The `.mjs` extension guarantees ESM regardless of `package.json` type.

### 3. Install dependencies
- **Core** (always): `npm install -D eslint @eslint/js`
- **TypeScript** (if `tsconfig.json` exists): `npm install -D typescript-eslint`
- **React** (if React project): `npm install -D eslint-plugin-react eslint-plugin-react-hooks eslint-plugin-react-refresh`
- **Next.js** (if Next.js project): `npm install -D @next/eslint-plugin-next eslint-plugin-react eslint-plugin-react-hooks`
- **Prettier** (recommended — always install unless user declines): `npm install -D eslint-config-prettier`
- **Import sorting** (optional, add if user wants): `npm install -D eslint-plugin-import`
- Run all installs in a single command to save time.

### 4. Create eslint.config.mjs
- Create `eslint.config.mjs` at the project root (same level as `package.json`).
- **CRITICAL**: Use `export default [...]` array syntax, NOT the legacy `module.exports` or `extends` pattern.

#### 4a. TypeScript + React (most common — Next.js and Vite projects)
```javascript
import js from '@eslint/js'
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint'
import react from 'eslint-plugin-react'
import reactHooks from 'eslint-plugin-react-hooks'
import prettier from 'eslint-config-prettier'

export default tseslint.config(
  {
    ignores: [
      'node_modules/',
      '.next/',
      'out/',
      'dist/',
      'build/',
      'coverage/',
      '*.config.js',
      '*.config.mjs',
    ],
  },
  js.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  {
    files: ['**/*.{ts,tsx,js,jsx}'],
    plugins: {
      react,
      'react-hooks': reactHooks,
    },
    rules: {
      ...reactHooks.configs.recommended.rules,
      'react/react-in-jsx-scope': 'off',
      'react/prop-types': 'off',
      '@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars': ['error', { argsIgnorePattern: '^_', varsIgnorePattern: '^_' }],
      '@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any': 'warn',
    },
    settings: {
      react: {
        version: 'detect',
      },
    },
  },
  prettier,
)
```

#### 4b. TypeScript only (Node.js / API projects)
```javascript
import js from '@eslint/js'
import tseslint from 'typescript-eslint'
import prettier from 'eslint-config-prettier'

export default tseslint.config(
  {
    ignores: ['node_modules/', 'dist/', 'build/', 'coverage/'],
  },
  js.configs.recommended,
  ...tseslint.configs.recommended,
  {
    files: ['**/*.{ts,js}'],
    rules: {
      '@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars': ['error', { argsIgnorePattern: '^_', varsIgnorePattern: '^_' }],
      '@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any': 'warn',
    },
  },
  prettier,
)
```

#### 4c. Next.js specific additions
- If using Next.js, add the Next.js plugin to the config:
```javascript
import nextPlugin from '@next/eslint-plugin-next'

// Add this object to the config array:
{
  plugins: {
    '@next/next': nextPlugin,
  },
  rules: {
    ...nextPlugin.configs.recommended.rules,
    ...nextPlugin.configs['core-web-vitals'].rules,
  },
}
```
- **NOTE**: `eslint-config-next` (the legacy Next.js ESLint config) does NOT natively support flat config. Use `@next/eslint-plugin-next` directly instead.

### 5. Update package.json scripts
- Add or update the lint script in `package.json`:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "eslint .",
    "lint:fix": "eslint . --fix"
  }
}
```
- If a Next.js project already has `"lint": "next lint"`, keep it but note that `next lint` may not fully support flat config yet. Add `"lint:eslint": "eslint ."` as an alternative.

### 6. Handle Prettier integration
- `eslint-config-prettier` MUST be the LAST item in the config array — it disables all formatting rules that conflict with Prettier.
- If the user has `eslint-plugin-prettier` installed, recommend removing it. The modern approach is to run Prettier separately, not as an ESLint rule. Running Prettier as an ESLint plugin is slow and produces confusing error messages.
- Verify `.prettierrc` or `prettier.config.*` exists if the user uses Prettier. If not, suggest creating one.

### 7. VS Code integration
- Check if `.vscode/settings.json` exists.
- Ensure ESLint extension settings use flat config:
```json
{
  "eslint.useFlatConfig": true,
  "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
    "source.fixAll.eslint": "explicit"
  }
}
```
- If `eslint.useFlatConfig` is missing, the VS Code ESLint extension may not detect the flat config file.

### 8. Verify the setup
- Run: `npx eslint .` to check for errors.
- Run: `npx eslint . --fix` to auto-fix what can be fixed.
- If there are errors about the config itself (not lint errors in code), the config file has issues.
- Verify no legacy config files remain that could cause confusion.
- Check that the `ignores` array in the config covers build output directories.

### 9. Type-checked linting (optional, for strict projects)
- For stricter TypeScript checking, use `tseslint.configs.recommendedTypeChecked` instead of `tseslint.configs.recommended`:
```javascript
...tseslint.configs.recommendedTypeChecked,
{
  languageOptions: {
    parserOptions: {
      projectService: true,
      tsconfigRootDir: import.meta.dirname,
    },
  },
},
```
- This enables rules that use TypeScript's type information (slower but catches more bugs).
- `import.meta.dirname` requires Node.js 21+. For older Node, use `import { dirname } from 'node:path'` and `import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'`.

### Common errors & fixes
- **"Key 'extends' is not valid in a flat config"** → Legacy syntax. Flat config uses spread (`...tseslint.configs.recommended`) or array concatenation, NOT `extends`.
- **"eslintrc configuration is not supported"** → A `.eslintrc.*` file is being picked up. Delete all legacy config files.
- **"Cannot find module 'eslint-config-...'"** → A legacy shareable config that doesn't support flat config. Find the flat config equivalent or use `@eslint/eslintrc` compatibility utility as a last resort.
- **"Unexpected top-level property 'env'"** → `env` is a legacy config property. In flat config, use `languageOptions.globals` instead. Install `globals` package: `npm install -D globals`, then `import globals from 'globals'` and add `languageOptions: { globals: { ...globals.browser, ...globals.node } }`.
- **"Unexpected top-level property 'plugins' (array)"** → In flat config, `plugins` is an object (`{ react }`) not an array (`['react']`).
- **Config not detected** → ESLint v9 looks for `eslint.config.mjs`, `.js`, or `.cjs`. If none found, it errors. Check the file name is exact.
- **"Parsing error: Unexpected token" on JSX** → Missing React plugin or `jsx: true` not set. Ensure `eslint-plugin-react` is configured.
- **next lint not working with flat config** → `next lint` uses its own ESLint wrapper that may not support flat config. Use `npx eslint .` directly instead.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT create both `.eslintrc.*` AND `eslint.config.mjs` — this causes undefined behavior.
- Do NOT use `extends` keyword in flat config — it does not exist. Use array spread or `tseslint.config()` helper.
- Do NOT use `plugins` as an array of strings — flat config requires plugin objects: `plugins: { react }` not `plugins: ['react']`.
- Do NOT use `env: { browser: true }` — flat config uses `languageOptions: { globals: { ...globals.browser } }`.
- Do NOT put `eslint-config-prettier` anywhere other than LAST in the config array — it must override all other rules.
- Do NOT install `eslint-plugin-prettier` for new projects — run Prettier separately.
- Do NOT use `require()` in `eslint.config.mjs` — use `import` syntax.
- Do NOT ignore the migration of custom rules from legacy config — always port them to the flat config format.

skills.title: Set up an Expo React Native project from scratch
skills.summary: Complete Expo setup flow — create-expo-app, Expo Router, native modules, EAS Build, env vars, OTA updates, and common pitfalls.
skills.declaration: I will set up a new Expo React Native project using the official Expo toolchain. If you prefer bare React Native CLI or a different template, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set Up Expo React Native Project

### 1. Create the project
- Use `npx create-expo-app@latest <project-name>` to scaffold.
- If user wants a specific template: `npx create-expo-app@latest <name> --template tabs` (or `blank`, `blank-typescript`).
- Default template uses Expo Router with file-based routing — confirm this is desired.
- `cd` into the project directory after creation.

### 2. Verify initial setup
- Run `npx expo start` to confirm the dev server launches.
- Confirm Expo Go connects on a physical device or simulator.
- If using a simulator: iOS requires Xcode + Simulator installed. Android requires Android Studio + an AVD configured.

### 3. Expo Router setup (if not using tabs template)
- Install: `npx expo install expo-router expo-linking expo-constants expo-status-bar`.
- Create `app/` directory at project root for file-based routes.
- Add entry point in `package.json`: `"main": "expo-router/entry"`.
- Create `app/_layout.tsx` as the root layout.
- Create `app/index.tsx` as the home screen.
- Add `scheme` to `app.json` for deep linking.

### 4. Native modules (Expo Modules)
- For camera, notifications, location, etc., use `npx expo install <package>`.
- Always prefer `expo install` over `npm install` — it pins SDK-compatible versions.
- Common modules: `expo-camera`, `expo-notifications`, `expo-location`, `expo-image-picker`, `expo-secure-store`, `expo-haptics`.
- If a native module requires a config plugin, add it to `app.json` under `"plugins"`.
- After adding native modules that need config plugins, run `npx expo prebuild` to regenerate native projects.

### 5. Environment variables
- Use `EXPO_PUBLIC_` prefix for client-side env vars (e.g., `EXPO_PUBLIC_API_URL`).
- Access via `process.env.EXPO_PUBLIC_API_URL` — works in Expo SDK 49+.
- For secrets (API keys that must not leak to the client), use EAS Secrets: `eas secret:create --name MY_SECRET --value xxx`.
- Never commit `.env` files. Add `.env*` to `.gitignore`.

### 6. EAS Build setup
- Install EAS CLI: `npm install -g eas-cli`.
- Login: `eas login`.
- Initialize: `eas build:configure` — generates `eas.json`.
- Build profiles in `eas.json`:
  - `development` — internal distribution, includes dev client.
  - `preview` — internal distribution, production-like but not store-ready.
  - `production` — store-ready build.
- Development build: `eas build --profile development --platform ios` (or `android`).
- Production build: `eas build --profile production --platform all`.
- For local builds (no EAS cloud): `eas build --local`.

### 7. OTA Updates (EAS Update)
- Install: `npx expo install expo-updates`.
- Configure in `app.json`: set `updates.url` and `runtimeVersion`.
- Run `eas update:configure` to set up.
- Publish an update: `eas update --branch production --message "description"`.
- OTA updates only work for JS/asset changes — native module changes require a new build.
- Use `runtimeVersion` policy `"appVersion"` or `"fingerprint"` to control compatibility.

### 8. Project structure recommendation
```
app/               # Expo Router screens
  _layout.tsx      # Root layout
  index.tsx        # Home
  (tabs)/          # Tab group
components/        # Reusable components
lib/               # Utilities, API clients
constants/         # Colors, config
assets/            # Images, fonts
```

### Common errors & fixes
- **Metro cache stale / weird errors after install** → `npx expo start --clear` or `rm -rf node_modules/.cache`.
- **iOS Simulator not found** → Open Xcode > Settings > Platforms > install iOS simulator runtime.
- **Android SDK not found** → Set `ANDROID_HOME` env var. Run `sdkmanager` to install platform tools.
- **'Unable to resolve module'** → Clear Metro cache. If persists, delete `node_modules` and reinstall.
- **'Invariant Violation: Native module cannot be null'** → You added a native module but didn't rebuild. Run `npx expo prebuild --clean` then `npx expo run:ios` or `run:android`.
- **EAS build fails with 'credentials'** → Run `eas credentials` to configure signing. iOS needs an Apple Developer account.
- **OTA update not appearing** → Check `runtimeVersion` matches between build and update. Mismatched versions silently skip updates.
- **Expo Go can't run native modules** → Expo Go only supports modules in the Expo SDK. For custom native code, use a development build (`eas build --profile development`).

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT use `npm install` for Expo SDK packages — always use `npx expo install` to get compatible versions.
- Do NOT eject (`expo eject` is deprecated). Use `npx expo prebuild` for native customization.
- Do NOT modify `ios/` or `android/` directories directly if using managed workflow — changes get overwritten by prebuild.
- Do NOT put secrets in `EXPO_PUBLIC_` vars — they are embedded in the JS bundle and visible to anyone.
- Do NOT skip the development build step and rely solely on Expo Go for apps with native modules.
- Do NOT run `eas build --profile production` without testing on a preview/development build first.
- Do NOT ignore `runtimeVersion` mismatches — they cause OTA updates to silently fail.

skills.title: Set up GitHub Actions CI for a Next.js project
skills.summary: Creates a production-grade GitHub Actions CI workflow for Next.js — build, lint, test, type-check with Node matrix, caching, concurrency groups, and path filters.
skills.declaration: I will create a GitHub Actions CI workflow (.github/workflows/ci.yml) for this Next.js project. The workflow will run build, lint, test, and type-check across Node 18/20/22 with dependency caching, concurrency groups, and path filters. If you already have a CI workflow or prefer a different CI provider (CircleCI, GitLab CI, etc.), say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set up GitHub Actions CI for Next.js

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Verify `package.json` exists and identify the package manager: check for `pnpm-lock.yaml` (pnpm), `yarn.lock` (yarn), or `package-lock.json` (npm). Use the detected package manager for ALL commands throughout this workflow.
- Identify existing scripts in `package.json`: look for `build`, `lint`, `test`, `type-check` (or `typecheck` or `tsc`). Record which scripts exist — only include CI steps for scripts that are actually defined.
- Check if `.github/workflows/` directory already exists. If a `ci.yml` already exists, ask the user before overwriting.
- Check `next.config.js` or `next.config.mjs` or `next.config.ts` for any special build configuration (e.g., `output: 'standalone'`, `output: 'export'`).
- Check if the project uses TypeScript by looking for `tsconfig.json`.

### 2. Detect environment variable requirements
- Scan for `process.env.` usage in the codebase (grep across `src/`, `app/`, `pages/`, `lib/`, `next.config.*`).
- Separate env vars into: (a) build-time vars (used in `next.config.*` or server components), (b) runtime-only vars, (c) public vars (`NEXT_PUBLIC_*`).
- For build-time vars that are required, they must be set in the workflow. Prepare a list.
- NEVER put actual secret values in the workflow file. Use `${{ secrets.VAR_NAME }}` syntax and instruct the user to add them in GitHub Settings > Secrets.

### 3. Create the workflow file
- Create `.github/workflows/` directory if it does not exist: `mkdir -p .github/workflows`.
- Write `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with the following structure:

```yaml
name: CI

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, master, develop]
    paths-ignore:
      - '**.md'
      - 'docs/**'
      - '.vscode/**'
      - 'LICENSE'
  push:
    branches: [main, master]
    paths-ignore:
      - '**.md'
      - 'docs/**'
      - '.vscode/**'
      - 'LICENSE'

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  lint:
    name: Lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      # Cache step — see step 4 below for package-manager-specific config
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: <install command>
      - name: Lint
        run: <lint command>

  typecheck:
    name: Type Check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Only include this job if TypeScript is used
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      # Cache step
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: <install command>
      - name: Type check
        run: <typecheck command>

  test:
    name: Test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Only include this job if a test script exists
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      # Cache step
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: <install command>
      - name: Run tests
        run: <test command>

  build:
    name: Build (Node ${{ matrix.node-version }})
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
      fail-fast: false
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
      # Cache step
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: <install command>
      - name: Build
        run: <build command>
        env:
          # Add build-time env vars here
          # NEXT_PUBLIC_*: placeholders are fine for public vars
          # Secret vars: ${{ secrets.VAR_NAME }}
```

**IMPORTANT template rules:**
- Replace `<install command>` with the detected package manager command (`npm ci`, `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, or `yarn install --frozen-lockfile`).
- Replace `<lint command>` with the actual script (`npm run lint`, `pnpm lint`, etc.).
- Replace `<typecheck command>` with the actual script. Common patterns: `npx tsc --noEmit`, `npm run type-check`, `npm run typecheck`.
- Replace `<test command>` with the actual test script.
- Replace `<build command>` with `npm run build` (or equivalent).
- OMIT jobs entirely if the corresponding script does not exist (e.g., no `test` script → no test job).
- Use `fail-fast: false` on the build matrix so all Node versions report results even if one fails.

### 4. Configure dependency caching (package-manager-specific)

**For npm:**
