skills.title: Set up Vitest for Next.js / React projects
skills.summary: Complete Vitest setup — installs dependencies, configures vitest.config.ts with React plugin and jsdom, sets up Testing Library, coverage, path aliases, and package.json scripts.
skills.declaration: Vitest is a Vite-native test runner that is fast, ESM-first, and has Jest-compatible APIs. I will set up Vitest for this project with React Testing Library. If you prefer Jest or another test framework, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions: ## Set Up Vitest for Next.js / React ### 1. Pre-flight checks - Verify `package.json` exists. - Check if any test framework is already installed: look for `jest`, `vitest`, `mocha`, or `@testing-library/*` in dependencies/devDependencies. - If Jest is already configured, ask the user: migrate to Vitest or keep Jest? Do NOT mix both in the same project. - Determine the project type: - **Next.js**: Check for `next.config.js`/`next.config.mjs`. - **Vite**: Check for `vite.config.ts`. - **CRA or other**: Check build tooling. - Read `tsconfig.json` to identify path aliases (e.g., `@/*` → `./src/*`). These MUST be mirrored in vitest config. ### 2. Install dependencies - Core: ``` npm i -D vitest @vitejs/plugin-react ``` - Test environment (choose one): - **jsdom** (recommended for most projects — wider compatibility): ``` npm i -D jsdom ``` - **happy-dom** (faster but less complete DOM implementation — use for speed-critical large test suites): ``` npm i -D happy-dom ``` - Default recommendation: use **jsdom** unless the user specifically requests happy-dom. - Testing Library (for React component tests): ``` npm i -D @testing-library/react @testing-library/jest-dom @testing-library/user-event ``` - Coverage: ``` npm i -D @vitest/coverage-v8 ``` ### 3. Create vitest.config.ts - Create `vitest.config.ts` at the project root. Do NOT put test config inside `vite.config.ts` for Next.js projects — keep them separate to avoid conflicts with Next.js build. - For Vite-based projects, you CAN extend the existing `vite.config.ts` using `mergeConfig` if preferred. #### For Next.js projects: ```typescript import { defineConfig } from 'vitest/config' import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react' import path from 'path' export default defineConfig({ plugins: [react()], test: { environment: 'jsdom', globals: true, setupFiles: ['./vitest.setup.ts'], include: ['**/*.{test,spec}.{ts,tsx,js,jsx}'], exclude: ['node_modules', '.next', 'e2e'], css: true, }, resolve: { alias: { '@': path.resolve(__dirname, './src'), }, }, }) ``` - CRITICAL: Read `tsconfig.json` `compilerOptions.paths` and replicate EVERY alias in `resolve.alias`. Mismatched aliases cause `Cannot find module '@/...'` errors. - If the project uses `src/` directory, alias `@` → `./src`. If no `src/`, alias `@` → `.`. - Adjust the alias map to match the actual tsconfig paths. #### For Vite projects: ```typescript /// import { defineConfig } from 'vite' import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react' import path from 'path' export default defineConfig({ plugins: [react()], test: { environment: 'jsdom', globals: true, setupFiles: ['./vitest.setup.ts'], include: ['**/*.{test,spec}.{ts,tsx,js,jsx}'], css: true, }, resolve: { alias: { '@': path.resolve(__dirname, './src'), }, }, }) ``` ### 4. Create vitest.setup.ts - Create `vitest.setup.ts` at the project root: ```typescript import '@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest' ``` - This registers custom matchers like `toBeInTheDocument()`, `toHaveTextContent()`, etc. - If the project needs additional setup (e.g., mocking `next/router`, `next/navigation`), add mocks here: ```typescript import '@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest' // Mock next/navigation for App Router projects vi.mock('next/navigation', () => ({ useRouter: () => ({ push: vi.fn(), replace: vi.fn(), back: vi.fn(), prefetch: vi.fn(), }), useSearchParams: () => new URLSearchParams(), usePathname: () => '/', })) ``` ### 5. Add TypeScript types - If `globals: true` is set, add Vitest types to `tsconfig.json`: ```json { "compilerOptions": { "types": ["vitest/globals"] } } ``` - This enables `describe`, `it`, `expect`, `vi` without explicit imports. - Do NOT remove existing types — append `"vitest/globals"` to the array. ### 6. Add package.json scripts - Add these scripts to `package.json` (do NOT overwrite existing scripts): ```json { "scripts": { "test": "vitest run", "test:watch": "vitest", "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage", "test:ui": "vitest --ui" } } ``` - `vitest run` = single run (CI-friendly). `vitest` = watch mode (development). - For Vitest UI, also install: `npm i -D @vitest/ui`. ### 7. Create an example test - Create a sample test to verify the setup works. Place it next to an existing component or in a `__tests__` directory. - Example for a React component: ```typescript import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react' import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest' import HomePage from '@/app/page' // adjust path describe('HomePage', () => { it('renders without crashing', () => { render() expect(document.body).toBeTruthy() }) }) ``` - If the component has complex dependencies (providers, context, fetch), create a simpler test first to validate the setup. ### 8. Run and verify - Run: `npx vitest run`. - If tests pass, the setup is complete. - If tests fail, check the error output carefully and apply fixes from the common errors section below. - Run coverage to verify: `npx vitest run --coverage`. ### 9. Testing API routes and Server Actions (Next.js) - API routes and Server Actions run in Node.js, not the browser. Test them with `environment: 'node'`. - Use per-file environment override with a comment at the top of the test file: ```typescript // @vitest-environment node import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest' ``` - For API routes, import the handler and test it directly: ```typescript // @vitest-environment node import { GET, POST } from '@/app/api/example/route' import { NextRequest } from 'next/server' describe('GET /api/example', () => { it('returns 200', async () => { const req = new NextRequest('http://localhost:3000/api/example') const res = await GET(req) expect(res.status).toBe(200) }) }) ``` - For Server Actions, import and call them directly. Mock any database or external calls. ### 10. Coverage configuration - To customize coverage, add to `vitest.config.ts`: ```typescript test: { coverage: { provider: 'v8', reporter: ['text', 'html', 'lcov'], exclude: [ 'node_modules/', '.next/', '**/*.config.*', '**/*.d.ts', 'vitest.setup.ts', ], thresholds: { statements: 80, branches: 80, functions: 80, lines: 80, }, }, } ``` - Add `coverage/` to `.gitignore`. ### Common errors & fixes - **'Cannot use import statement outside a module'** → Missing `@vitejs/plugin-react` plugin in vitest config. Ensure `plugins: [react()]` is present. - **'Cannot find module @/...'** → Path alias mismatch. Compare `tsconfig.json` `paths` with `resolve.alias` in vitest config. They MUST match exactly. - **'document is not defined'** or **'window is not defined'** → Missing `environment: 'jsdom'` (or `'happy-dom'`). The default environment is `node` which has no DOM. - **'toBeInTheDocument is not a function'** → Missing setup file. Ensure `setupFiles: ['./vitest.setup.ts']` is set and the file imports `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest`. - **'ReferenceError: describe is not defined'** → Either set `globals: true` in vitest config, or explicitly import `{ describe, it, expect }` from `'vitest'`. - **'TypeError: vi.fn is not a function'** → Same as above — `vi` needs `globals: true` or explicit import. - **CSS/SCSS import errors** → Add `css: true` to test config, or mock CSS files: ```typescript // vitest.config.ts test: { css: true, // or css: { modules: { classNameStrategy: 'non-scoped' } } } ``` - **Next.js Image component errors** → Mock `next/image` in setup file: ```typescript vi.mock('next/image', () => ({ default: (props: any) => , })) ``` - **Slow tests** → Switch from `jsdom` to `happy-dom` for faster execution. Or use `--pool=forks` for CPU-heavy tests. - **Coverage shows 0%** → Ensure `@vitest/coverage-v8` is installed. Check that test files are not in the `exclude` list. ### What NOT to do - Do NOT use Jest config syntax (`jest.config.js`, `moduleNameMapper`, `transform`) in vitest config — Vitest has its own API. - Do NOT skip setting `environment: 'jsdom'` — the default is `node`, which breaks ALL DOM/React tests silently. - Do NOT mix Jest and Vitest in the same project — they conflict on globals, matchers, and mock APIs. - Do NOT put vitest config inside `next.config.js` — it does not belong there. - Do NOT use `@testing-library/jest-dom` without the `/vitest` import path — use `@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest` for proper Vitest matcher integration. - Do NOT forget to add `vitest/globals` to `tsconfig.json` types when using `globals: true` — TypeScript will show errors for `describe`, `it`, etc. - Do NOT create test files with `.test.js` extension in a TypeScript project — use `.test.ts` or `.test.tsx` for consistency. - Do NOT ignore path alias setup — this is the #1 cause of 'module not found' errors in Vitest with Next.js.

skills.title: Set Up Zod Schema Validation with React Hook Form
skills.summary: Zod schema validation setup with React Hook Form integration — type-safe forms, resolver config, reusable schemas, and server-side validation.
skills.declaration: I will set up Zod schema validation integrated with React Hook Form in this project. This includes installing dependencies, creating reusable validation schemas, configuring the hookform/resolvers bridge, and adding server-side validation. I will NOT use untyped form handling or skip server-side validation. If you need a different form library (Formik, etc.) or validation approach, say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set Up Zod Schema Validation with React Hook Form

### 1. Install dependencies
- Run `npm install zod react-hook-form @hookform/resolvers`.
- Verify all three packages appear in `package.json` dependencies.
- `zod` is the schema validation library. `react-hook-form` handles form state. `@hookform/resolvers` bridges the two.

### 2. Create a validation schemas directory
- Create `lib/validations/` (or `src/lib/validations/` if using `src/` directory).
- This directory holds all reusable Zod schemas. One file per domain entity is recommended.

### 3. Create your first Zod schema
- Create `lib/validations/user.ts` as an example schema file.
- Define a schema with `z.object({})` — give each field explicit validation rules.
- Export both the schema and the inferred TypeScript type using `z.infer<typeof schema>`.
- Include meaningful error messages using `.min()`, `.max()`, `.email()`, `.regex()` with custom message strings.
- Example fields to demonstrate: email (z.string().email()), password (z.string().min(8)), confirmPassword, name (z.string().min(1)).
- Add a `.refine()` or `.superRefine()` to demonstrate cross-field validation (e.g., password === confirmPassword).

### 4. Create a form component using the schema
- Create a component (e.g., `components/UserForm.tsx`) with `'use client'` directive.
- Import `useForm` from `react-hook-form` and `zodResolver` from `@hookform/resolvers/zod`.
- Import your Zod schema and its inferred type.
- Call `useForm` with `resolver: zodResolver(yourSchema)` and type parameter matching the inferred type.
- Destructure `register`, `handleSubmit`, `formState: { errors }` from the hook.
- Wire each input field using `{...register('fieldName')}`.
- Display field-level errors from `errors.fieldName?.message`.
- The `onSubmit` handler receives fully validated, typed data — no additional parsing needed.

### 5. Handle nested and array schemas
- For nested objects, use `z.object({})` inside another `z.object({})`.
- For arrays, use `z.array(z.object({...}))` and pair with `useFieldArray` from react-hook-form.
- For optional fields, use `.optional()` or `.nullable()`. Understand the difference: `.optional()` allows `undefined`, `.nullable()` allows `null`.

### 6. Set up server-side validation
- In your Server Action or API route, import the same Zod schema used on the client.
- Call `schema.safeParse(data)` — NEVER use `.parse()` directly in server code without try/catch.
- `safeParse` returns `{ success: boolean, data?, error? }` — check `success` before using `data`.
- If validation fails, return `result.error.flatten()` to get structured field errors suitable for displaying in the UI.
- This ensures the same validation rules apply on both client and server — a single source of truth.

### 7. Create reusable schema fragments
- For fields used across multiple schemas (e.g., email, phone), create `lib/validations/common.ts`.
- Export individual field schemas: `export const emailSchema = z.string().email('Invalid email')`.
- Compose schemas using spread or `.merge()`: `const formSchema = z.object({ email: emailSchema, ...otherFields })`.
- Use `.extend()` to add fields to an existing schema, `.pick()` to select specific fields, `.omit()` to exclude fields.

### 8. Add transform and preprocess utilities
- Use `.transform()` to normalize data after validation (e.g., `.transform(v => v.trim().toLowerCase())` on email).
- Use `z.preprocess()` to coerce input types before validation (e.g., converting string to number from form inputs).
- For HTML number inputs that return strings, use `z.coerce.number()` instead of manual preprocessing.

### 9. Integrate with shadcn/ui Form components (if applicable)
- If the project uses shadcn/ui, use the `<Form>`, `<FormField>`, `<FormItem>`, `<FormLabel>`, `<FormControl>`, `<FormMessage>` components.
- Pass the `useForm` return value to `<Form {...form}>`.
- Use `<FormField control={form.control} name="fieldName" render={({field}) => ...} />`.
- `<FormMessage />` automatically displays Zod validation errors for that field.

### 10. Verify the setup
- Start the dev server: `npm run dev`.
- Submit the form with invalid data — confirm field-level errors display correctly.
- Submit with valid data — confirm the typed data reaches the submit handler.
- Test server-side validation separately by sending raw POST requests with invalid data.
- Confirm TypeScript catches type mismatches between the schema and form fields at build time.

### Common Errors & Fixes
- **"resolver is not a function"** — You imported `zodResolver` from the wrong path. The correct import is `from '@hookform/resolvers/zod'`, NOT `from '@hookform/resolvers'`.
- **Form not showing errors** — Ensure you are reading from `formState.errors`, not calling `getFieldState`. Also check that `mode` is set (default is `'onSubmit'` — errors only show after first submit attempt).
- **Type mismatch between schema and useForm** — Pass the inferred type as a generic to `useForm<YourType>()`. The type must match the schema output, not input (matters when using `.transform()`).
- **Server validation passes but client fails (or vice versa)** — You are using different schemas. Import the SAME schema in both places.
- **"Expected string, received undefined"** — HTML inputs return empty strings, not undefined. Use `z.string().min(1, 'Required')` instead of `z.string()` alone for required fields — `.string()` alone accepts empty strings.
- **Number fields always fail** — HTML inputs always return strings. Use `z.coerce.number()` to auto-convert, or `z.preprocess(Number, z.number())`.
- **Refine errors not showing on specific fields** — `.refine()` at the object level creates root-level errors. Use `.superRefine()` with `ctx.addIssue({ path: ['fieldName'], ... })` to attach errors to specific fields.

### What NOT to Do
- Do NOT use `.parse()` on the server without try/catch — it throws on invalid input. Always use `.safeParse()` in server code.
- Do NOT define validation schemas inline in components — extract them to separate files for reuse and testing.
- Do NOT skip server-side validation because you have client-side validation. Client validation is a UX feature, not a security feature.
- Do NOT use `any` as the form type — the entire point of Zod + React Hook Form is end-to-end type safety.
- Do NOT put Zod schemas in `'use client'` files — schemas are plain objects and should be importable from both client and server code.
- Do NOT forget `.min(1)` on required string fields — `z.string()` alone considers empty string valid.

skills.title: Set Up Zustand State Management
skills.summary: Zustand store setup — typed stores, slices pattern, persist middleware, devtools, and SSR-safe hydration for Next.js.
skills.declaration: I will set up Zustand state management in this project. This includes installing the package, creating typed stores, configuring middleware (persist, devtools), and handling SSR hydration for Next.js. I will NOT use untyped stores or skip SSR hydration handling. If you need a different state management approach (Redux, Jotai, etc.), say so before I begin.
skills.instructions:
## Set Up Zustand State Management

### 1. Install dependencies
- Run `npm install zustand`.
- Verify `zustand` appears in `package.json` dependencies.
- Zustand has zero peer dependencies — no additional packages needed for basic usage.

### 2. Create the store directory
- Create `stores/` (or `src/stores/` if using `src/` directory).
- One file per store. Name files by domain: `stores/use-auth-store.ts`, `stores/use-cart-store.ts`, etc.
- Convention: prefix store files with `use-` since they export hooks.

### 3. Create a basic typed store
- Create `stores/use-counter-store.ts` as a starter example.
- Define a TypeScript interface for the store state AND actions together.
- Use `create<StoreInterface>()` with the full type parameter.
- Separate state (data) from actions (functions) in the interface for clarity.
- Return both state and actions from the store creator function.
- Export the store as a named export: `export const useCounterStore = create<CounterStore>()(...)` — note the double parentheses `()()` when using TypeScript.

### 4. Use the store in components
- Import the store hook in any component: `import { useCounterStore } from '@/stores/use-counter-store'`.
- Select only the state you need using a selector: `const count = useCounterStore((state) => state.count)` — this prevents unnecessary re-renders.
- For multiple values, use separate selectors or `useShallow`: `import { useShallow } from 'zustand/react/shallow'` then `const { count, increment } = useCounterStore(useShallow((state) => ({ count: state.count, increment: state.increment })))`.
- NEVER destructure the entire store without a selector: `const store = useCounterStore()` — this causes re-renders on ANY state change.

### 5. Add persist middleware
- For stores that need persistence (user preferences, cart, etc.), wrap with `persist`.
- Import: `import { persist } from 'zustand/middleware'`.
- Wrap the creator: `create<StoreType>()(persist((set) => ({...}), { name: 'store-name' }))`.
- The `name` is the localStorage key. Choose it carefully — it must be unique across your app.
- Use `partialize` option to persist only specific fields: `partialize: (state) => ({ theme: state.theme })` — do NOT persist functions or computed values.
- For SSR apps (Next.js), persisted stores cause hydration mismatches. See Step 7.

### 6. Add devtools middleware
- Import: `import { devtools } from 'zustand/middleware'`.
- Wrap the creator: `create<StoreType>()(devtools((set) => ({...}), { name: 'StoreName' }))`.
- For combined middleware, nest them: `create<StoreType>()(devtools(persist((set) => ({...}), { name: 'store-key' }), { name: 'StoreName' }))`.
- Order matters: `devtools` should be the outermost wrapper.
- Install the Redux DevTools browser extension to inspect state changes.

### 7. Handle SSR hydration (Next.js)
- Persisted stores read from localStorage on mount, causing a hydration mismatch with server-rendered HTML.
- Create a hydration component `components/StoreHydration.tsx` or use the `onRehydrateStorage` callback.
- Recommended approach: add a `hasHydrated` flag to persisted stores and check it before rendering dependent UI.
- Add to your store: `hasHydrated: false` in state, and in the persist config add `onRehydrateStorage: () => () => { useStore.setState({ hasHydrated: true }) }`.
- In components, check `hasHydrated` before rendering persisted values, showing a loading state or skeleton while hydrating.
- Alternative: use `skipHydration: true` in persist config and manually call `useStore.persist.rehydrate()` in a useEffect.

### 8. Implement the slices pattern for large stores
- For complex state, split into slices that compose into a single store.
- Create each slice as a function: `const createAuthSlice: StateCreator<FullStoreType, [], [], AuthSlice> = (set) => ({...})`.
- Combine slices in the root store: `create<FullStoreType>()((...a) => ({ ...createAuthSlice(...a), ...createCartSlice(...a) }))`.
- Each slice can access the full store via `get()` parameter: `(set, get) => ({...})`.
- Type the full store as the intersection of all slice types.

### 9. Add computed/derived values
- Zustand does not have built-in computed values like MobX.
- Option A: compute inline in the selector: `const total = useCartStore((state) => state.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.price, 0))`.
- Option B: for expensive computations, create a memoized selector outside the component using a library like `reselect` or store the computed value.
- Option C: use `subscribeWithSelector` middleware and update a derived field when source fields change.

### 10. Verify the setup
- Start the dev server: `npm run dev`.
- Interact with components that use the store — confirm state updates and re-renders work correctly.
- Open Redux DevTools — confirm store state is visible and actions are logged.
- For persisted stores: refresh the page and confirm state persists.
- For SSR: view page source — confirm server-rendered HTML does not contain persisted client-only state.
- Run `npm run build` — confirm no TypeScript errors in store files.

### Common Errors & Fixes
- **Infinite re-render loop** — You are calling a state setter inside the component body without useEffect or an event handler. State updates must be inside callbacks or effects.
- **Component not re-rendering on state change** — You are not using a selector, or the selector returns a new object reference every render. Use `useShallow` for object/array selectors.
- **Hydration mismatch in Next.js** — Your persisted store renders client-only state on the server. Add the `hasHydrated` pattern from Step 7.
- **TypeScript error: "Argument of type ... is not assignable"** — You forgot the double parentheses pattern. With TypeScript, use `create<Type>()(...)` not `create<Type>(...)`.
- **Persist middleware not saving** — Check that `name` is set and localStorage is available. In incognito/private mode, localStorage may be restricted.
- **Devtools not showing store** — Ensure the Redux DevTools browser extension is installed. Zustand devtools use the same protocol.
- **Actions not updating state** — You must use the `set` function: `set({ count: get().count + 1 })` or `set((state) => ({ count: state.count + 1 }))`. Do NOT mutate state directly.

### What NOT to Do
- Do NOT destructure the entire store without a selector — `useStore()` without arguments causes re-renders on every state change.
- Do NOT put Zustand stores in `'use client'` files that also export Server Components. Stores are client-only; keep them in dedicated files.
- Do NOT persist entire stores with functions — use `partialize` to select only data fields.
- Do NOT create stores inside components — stores must be created at module scope (top level). Creating inside components causes a new store on every render.
- Do NOT use `useState` or `useReducer` alongside Zustand for the same piece of state — pick one source of truth.
- Do NOT forget to handle SSR hydration for persisted stores in Next.js — it causes visible content flashes and React hydration errors.

scars.title: Frame-diffing testsrc2 output proves nothing: the test source itself animates
scars.summary: Agent verifies a video transform by diffing two output frames rendered from testsrc2; testsrc2 is animated, so the diff is nonzero whether or not the transform works.
scars.mistake: To prove a time-varying filter (zoom pulse) was active, the agent applied it to testsrc2, extracted two frames, and treated a nonzero pixel diff as confirmation. testsrc2 (like testsrc and most lavfi demo sources) animates on its own, so any two frames differ regardless of the filter under test — the check can only ever pass, i.e. it is a false-positive generator.
scars.instead: Render the filter over a truly static source: 'color=c=black,drawgrid=w=40:h=40:t=2:c=white' or '-loop 1 -i still.png'. Then run a control pair FIRST: two frames where the effect should be absent must diff to exactly 0 (mean and max). Only after the control passes, measure the effect pair and require a clearly nonzero diff. A verification that cannot fail is not a verification.

scars.title: Auth.js v5 signIn/signOut fails because server and client imports are swapped
scars.summary: Importing signIn from next-auth/react in a server component (or from @/auth in a client component) causes runtime errors.
scars.mistake: Importing `signIn` or `signOut` from `next-auth/react` in a Server Component or Server Action. Auth.js v5 has TWO separate signIn/signOut exports: server-side from `@/auth` (the project's auth config file) and client-side from `next-auth/react`. Using the client import on the server throws 'signIn is not a function' or a React hook error. Using the server import on the client throws a module resolution error or attempts to run server-only code in the browser.
scars.instead: In Server Components and Server Actions, import from the project's auth config: `import { signIn, signOut } from '@/auth'`. In Client Components (files with 'use client'), import from the React package: `import { signIn, signOut } from 'next-auth/react'`. Never cross these boundaries. Additionally, do not wrap server-side signIn/signOut in try/catch — they intentionally throw a NEXT_REDIRECT which must propagate.

scars.title: Fixed full-screen drawer collapses into the header instead of covering the viewport
scars.summary: A position:fixed inset:0 drawer nested inside a header with backdrop-filter gets sized to the header box, not the viewport, because filter/backdrop-filter/transform ancestors become the containing block for fixed descendants.
scars.mistake: The agent built a mobile drawer as position:fixed; inset:0 inside a sticky header that had backdrop-filter:blur(10px). Per CSS spec, backdrop-filter (like filter, transform, perspective, will-change:transform) makes that ancestor the containing block for fixed-position descendants. The 'full-screen' overlay was silently laid out inside the ~70px header strip: its background covered only the header band and its links overflowed on top of the page content. Screenshots looked like a z-index or opacity bug, hiding the real cause.
scars.instead: Before implementing a fixed overlay, walk its ancestor chain for containing-block creators: in DevTools run [...ancestors].map(el => getComputedStyle(el)).find(cs => cs.backdropFilter !== 'none' || cs.filter !== 'none' || cs.transform !== 'none'). If the header needs blur, either (a) disable it in the mobile media query and use a solid rgba background instead, or (b) move the drawer node out to <body>. Verify numerically, not visually: assert drawer.getBoundingClientRect() equals {top:0, left:0, width:innerWidth, height:innerHeight} when open.

scars.title: CSS fix verified via MCP browser appears not applied — stylesheet served from cache
scars.summary: chrome-devtools MCP navigate_page type:url does not bypass subresource cache even with ignoreCache:true; edited CSS/JS keeps loading from cache and the agent misdiagnoses its own fix as broken. Only type:reload honors ignoreCache.
scars.mistake: After editing a stylesheet, the agent re-verified with navigate_page type:url + ignoreCache:true and found the change absent from computed styles. The document was re-fetched but CSS came from the HTTP cache (the local dev server sent Last-Modified, and Chrome's heuristic caching kept the old body). The agent nearly went hunting for a CSS bug that did not exist — document.styleSheets showed the old rule while fetch(url, {cache:'no-store'}) returned the new file.
scars.instead: After changing CSS/JS, re-verify with navigate_page type:reload + ignoreCache:true on the already-open page (this is the hard-reload path that flushes subresources). When a fix 'did not apply', settle it in one evaluate_script: compare the live rule (for (const s of document.styleSheets) for (const r of s.cssRules) ...) against fetch(cssUrl, {cache:'no-store'}).then(r=>r.text()) — if they differ, it is caching, not your CSS. Serving dev files with Cache-Control: no-store avoids the class entirely.

scars.title: Clerk middleware placed in wrong directory — auth silently does nothing
scars.summary: Agent puts middleware.ts inside app/ directory instead of project root or src/. Clerk auth appears to work in dev but silently fails to protect routes.
scars.mistake: The agent creates `app/middleware.ts` instead of `middleware.ts` (project root) or `src/middleware.ts`. Next.js only reads middleware from the root or src/ — placing it inside `app/` means it's never executed. The app loads without errors, login pages appear to work, but protected routes are not actually protected. The agent doesn't notice because there's no error message — the middleware simply never fires.
scars.instead: ALWAYS place middleware.ts at the project root (`./middleware.ts`) or inside `src/` if using the src directory pattern (`./src/middleware.ts`). NEVER place it inside `app/`. After creating the file, verify it's active by checking that accessing a protected route without auth redirects to the sign-in page. The middleware matcher in the config must also cover the routes you want to protect.

scars.title: Every nonexistent URL on a Cloudflare Pages site returns 200 with the homepage HTML
scars.summary: Without a root 404.html, Cloudflare Pages serves single-page-app fallback: any unknown path (including /robots.txt, /sitemap.xml, /favicon.ico) returns HTTP 200 with index.html, creating soft-404s that SEO tools and auditors flag.
scars.mistake: The agent deployed a static multi-page site with wrangler pages deploy and assumed missing paths would 404. Pages defaults to SPA fallback mode when no 404.html exists at the build output root: /any-garbage-path/ returned 200 text/html with the homepage, and probes for robots.txt / sitemap.xml / favicon.ico all appeared to 'exist' as 200 text/html, masking that they were absent. An external audit flagged soft-404s and missing crawl files.
scars.instead: Ship a 404.html at the output root — its presence alone switches Pages from SPA fallback to static 404 mode. After deploy, verify with: curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://site/definitely-not-a-page/ (must be 404). Also place real robots.txt and sitemap.xml; if .txt files come back as text/html, add a _headers file (e.g. '/robots.txt' + '  Content-Type: text/plain; charset=utf-8'). Note the CDN may negative-cache a recent 404 for the root favicon — verify against the *.pages.dev deployment URL or with a cache-busting query before concluding the file is missing.

scars.title: Credential-holding CI jobs shipped without ref gate, tag-ancestry check, or pinned toolchain
scars.summary: A deploy job with cloud secrets and a tag-triggered npm publish job with OIDC went to main missing three standard gates. An automated security review caught it post-merge; each gap was a one-line fix.
scars.mistake: Wrote a workflow_dispatch deploy job and a tag-triggered publish job that hold secrets/id-token, forgetting that (1) workflow_dispatch executes the workflow definition from WHATEVER ref is chosen — a modified copy on any branch can print the secrets; (2) any v-tag, even on an unreviewed commit, would publish to the registry; (3) `npm install -g npm@latest` inside the credential-holding job is an unpinned supply-chain edge.
scars.instead: Before merging any workflow whose job holds a secret or id-token, check three gates: (1) the job carries `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'`; (2) tag-triggered publishes verify `git merge-base --is-ancestor $GITHUB_SHA origin/main` (full-history checkout); (3) every toolchain install in that job is pinned to an exact version AND its artifact checksum is verified before execution (sha256sum -c; use curl -fsSL so a failed download dies loudly). Review these BEFORE merge — security review after merge means the window was open.

scars.title: After a domain cutover, agent misdiagnoses stale local-resolver responses as a broken server config
scars.summary: curl uses the system resolver; a router/ISP cache holding pre-cutover records makes a finished migration look like a failed deploy, sending the agent into pointless server-side churn.
scars.mistake: After switching a custom domain to a new host (e.g. Cloudflare Pages), plain `curl https://www.example.com` kept returning the OLD host's error page (Vercel 402 DEPLOYMENT_DISABLED). The agent assumed the new DNS records or the Pages custom-domain binding were wrong and burned time re-creating DNS records, polling for 'propagation', and re-checking the hosting config. The real cause: the local WiFi router / ISP resolver still cached pre-cutover records (old CNAME to the previous host) from before a nameserver change. The authoritative NS and public resolvers (Cloudflare, Google) had been correct the whole time — curl simply asks the system resolver, so local checks do not represent global state.
scars.instead: Verify a DNS/domain cutover with three checks before touching server config: (1) authoritative NS directly: `dig www.example.com @<assigned-ns>`, (2) a public resolver: `dig www.example.com @one.one.one.one`, (3) the new edge directly, bypassing DNS: `curl --resolve www.example.com:443:<edge-ip> https://www.example.com/`. If all three are correct, the migration is done and any local anomaly is resolver cache (wait for TTL; `resolvectl query`/`resolvectl flush-caches` on Linux only fixes the local layer, not the router's). Use `curl -v` and read the 'Connected to' line to see which origin IP actually answered — a response header like `server: Vercel` vs `server: cloudflare` identifies the layer instantly.

scars.title: Single-stage Dockerfile produces bloated image with devDependencies
scars.summary: Agent writes a single-stage Dockerfile that includes node_modules, devDependencies, and source files, resulting in an image over 1GB.
scars.mistake: Agent writes a Dockerfile with a single FROM stage that runs npm install (without --omit=dev), copies the entire project including source, tests, and devDependencies into the final image. The resulting image is 1GB+ instead of ~150MB. Deploy times balloon, CI caches miss constantly, and container startup is slower. The agent reports 'Docker image built successfully' without checking the image size.
scars.instead: Use a multi-stage Dockerfile. Stage 1 (builder): install all dependencies and build the project. Stage 2 (production): use a slim base image (e.g., node:20-alpine), copy only the dist/ or build output and production dependencies from the builder stage. Always add a .dockerignore to exclude node_modules, .git, tests, and docs. After building, run 'docker images' to verify the final image size is under 300MB for a typical Node.js app.

scars.title: Expo native module crashes because npm install pulled an SDK-incompatible version
scars.summary: Using npm install instead of npx expo install for Expo SDK packages installs incompatible versions that crash at runtime.
scars.mistake: Running `npm install expo-camera` (or any Expo SDK package) directly instead of `npx expo install expo-camera`. npm pulls the latest published version which may be incompatible with the project's Expo SDK version. The install succeeds, the build may succeed, but the app crashes at runtime with 'Invariant Violation: Native module cannot be null' or similar native bridge errors. The agent sees a clean install and moves on.
scars.instead: Always use `npx expo install <package>` for any Expo SDK package (expo-camera, expo-notifications, expo-location, expo-image-picker, etc.). This command reads the project's Expo SDK version from app.json/package.json and pins the compatible version of each package. After adding packages that need config plugins, also run `npx expo prebuild --clean` to regenerate native projects.

scars.title: ffmpeg crop w/h expressions evaluate once at init, so t-based zoom via crop fails
scars.summary: Agent builds a time-varying zoom with t in crop's w/h expressions; those are evaluated once at configure time where t=NAN, so the graph errors out (or silently stays static).
scars.mistake: To pulse-zoom a video, the agent writes crop=w='iw/zf(t)':h='ih/zf(t)' followed by scale back to the original size, assuming crop re-evaluates all expressions per frame. In ffmpeg only crop's x/y are per-frame; w/h are evaluated once at filter init where t=NAN, producing 'Error when evaluating the expression' at configure time (and if the expression happened to be NAN-safe, a permanently static crop instead of animation).
scars.instead: Use zoompan for time-varying zoom: zoompan=z='if(isnan(it),1,ZF(it))':x='iw/2-(iw/zoom/2)':y='ih/2-(ih/zoom/2)':d=1:s=WxH:fps=N — its z expression is evaluated per frame and in_time (it) carries the input timestamp (guard NAN on the first frames). scale=eval=frame also re-evaluates per frame, but a per-frame output size change forces constant graph reinit downstream; avoid it for this purpose.

scars.title: git push --delete with many refs is per-ref, not atomic — nonzero exit misread as total failure
scars.summary: A multi-ref `git push --delete` exits nonzero if ANY ref fails, but the other refs were still deleted; retry logic then reports 'remote ref does not exist' for every already-deleted ref, making a successful cleanup look like a total failure.
scars.mistake: Deleting ~150 remote branches in chunks of 25 with `git push origin --delete ref1 ref2 ...`, the agent gated on the command's exit code and fell back to per-ref retries when a chunk exited nonzero. Every retry printed FAIL ('remote ref does not exist') — a wall of 149 FAILs — because the chunk pushes HAD deleted the refs: ref updates in a multi-ref push are independent, and a single rejected ref makes the whole command exit nonzero. The agent nearly reported a fully successful cleanup as failed.
scars.instead: Treat any multi-ref push (including --delete) as per-ref: nonzero exit means at least one ref failed, never that all did. Parse the per-ref result lines ('- [deleted]' vs '! [rejected]') instead of the exit code, or skip output-parsing entirely and verify the END STATE: `git fetch --prune` then count `git branch -r`, or list branches via the hosting API. For mass deletions the final state is the verdict — exit codes along the way are noise.

scars.title: GitHub Actions workflow fails silently because repository secrets are not configured
scars.summary: Agent creates a GitHub Actions workflow referencing secrets that do not exist in the repository, causing deploys and tests to fail with cryptic errors.
scars.mistake: Agent writes a GitHub Actions workflow YAML that references ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}, ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}, or similar secrets, but never verifies that these secrets are actually configured in the repository's Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions. The workflow runs, gets empty strings or 'undefined' for every secret, and fails with vague errors like 'invalid API key', 'ECONNREFUSED', or 'authentication failed'. The agent sees the workflow file is syntactically correct and reports done.
scars.instead: After creating or modifying a GitHub Actions workflow, list every ${{ secrets.* }} reference in the YAML. For each one, verify it exists in the repository settings via 'gh secret list' or the GitHub UI under Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions. If any are missing, instruct the user to add them before pushing the workflow. Add a comment block at the top of the workflow YAML listing all required secrets and their descriptions.

scars.title: headless=new clamps --window-size below 500px, so mobile screenshots lie
scars.summary: Agent verifies mobile layout with --window-size=390 screenshots, but Chrome headless=new clamps the window to a 500px minimum (485px layout after scrollbar), so JS reads a fake width and overlays desync from the screenshot.
scars.mistake: Agent runs google-chrome --headless=new --window-size=390,H --screenshot to verify mobile layout. Chrome silently clamps the window to a ~500px minimum; the internal layout viewport becomes ~485px (500 minus scrollbar) while the screenshot is still emitted at 390px. window.innerWidth/document.documentElement.clientWidth report the clamped value, so any JS that positions absolutely-sized overlays (SVG viewBox, canvas, coordinate-computed decorations) builds them for the wrong width. The agent then misdiagnoses the resulting misalignment as an implementation bug and burns review cycles, because the same page is correct in real browsers.
scars.instead: Never use --window-size for sub-500px verification. Use CDP device emulation instead (e.g. chrome-devtools MCP: emulate viewport '390x844x1,mobile' then take a fullPage screenshot), which sets a true 390px layout viewport. In page code, read width from document.body.getBoundingClientRect().width (CSS truth) rather than clientWidth, and pin overlay SVG width/height attributes AND inline style to the same pixel values as the viewBox so no preserveAspectRatio scaling can occur. To confirm what headless actually did, run --dump-dom and inspect the generated viewBox/width values.

scars.title: Health check gated on a guessed command name with output suppressed — 'could not run' reported as 'failed'
scars.summary: Agent wires a pass/fail verdict to `guessed-command >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo FAIL`; the command name was wrong, so a 'command/script not found' error silently became a FAIL verdict against a healthy target.
scars.mistake: To verify generated artifacts were fresh, the agent ran `npm run gen-stats -- --check >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo MISMATCH`. The npm script name was guessed and did not exist (the real entry point was `node scripts/gen-stats.mjs --check`), so npm exited nonzero with 'Missing script' — but the suppressed output turned that into a fake MISMATCH verdict, briefly indicting a healthy repo. This is the inverse of the classic pipe-swallow: instead of a false PASS from a masked failure, a false FAIL from a check that never ran.
scars.instead: Never wire a pass/fail verdict to a command you have not seen succeed at least once with visible output. First run it bare (stdout+stderr shown) to confirm the tool exists and what success looks like; only then wrap it in gating. And keep the two verdicts distinguishable forever: 'the check could not run' (missing command, bad flag, no such file) must produce a different message than 'the check ran and failed' — e.g. `cmd; rc=$?; [ $rc -eq 127 ] && echo CHECK_BROKEN || ...`. When a gate trips, print the raw error before labeling the target broken.

scars.title: Adding width/height attributes to img silently disables CSS aspect-ratio
scars.summary: Agent adds width/height attributes for CLS, which fixes both axes and makes the CSS aspect-ratio rule inert, squashing the image into a stripe.
scars.mistake: To improve CLS, the agent adds intrinsic width/height attributes to an that is styled with width:100% and a CSS aspect-ratio (e.g. 4/5) + object-fit:cover. The height attribute now acts as the used height (both dimensions are determined), so aspect-ratio no longer applies: the image renders as a e.g. 400x1200 stripe with huge empty space. Because the attribute edit 'should not change visuals', the agent skips re-rendering the page and ships the regression; it is only caught later by an external reviewer measuring the rendered box.
scars.instead: Whenever you add width/height attributes to styled images, also set height:auto in CSS on those images (e.g. .fig img{width:100%;height:auto}) so aspect-ratio/object-fit keep working while the attributes still reserve layout space pre-load. Treat attribute additions as visual changes: re-render and measure the element's getBoundingClientRect (or compare width/height ratio) after the edit.

scars.title: Infinite lavfi source + output-side -t + sparse select makes ffmpeg run forever
scars.summary: With an infinite lavfi source, -t as an output option only stops when output pts reaches the limit; a select filter that passes few frames means that never happens, so ffmpeg consumes the source indefinitely.
scars.mistake: Agent runs: ffmpeg -f lavfi -i color=... -t 26.5 -vf "...,select='eq(n,300)+eq(n,771)'" out_%d.png. The color source is infinite and -t placed after -i is an OUTPUT limit measured on output timestamps. After the last selected frame, nothing reaches the output anymore, so the 26.5s output limit is never hit and ffmpeg reads the infinite source forever; the final selected frame can also sit buffered and unwritten. It looks like a hang and gets killed minutes later.
scars.instead: Put -t on the INPUT side (-t 26.5 BEFORE -i) so the source itself ends at that duration; on EOF the graph flushes and all selected frames are written. Alternatively bound the stream inside the graph with trim=end=26.5. Apply the same rule to every infinite lavfi source (color, testsrc*, anullsrc, sine) feeding frame-dropping filters.

scars.title: Mechanical conflict resolution silently dropped a code block — merged code did not parse
scars.summary: Conflicts from two parallel branches touching the same file were resolved mechanically (regex over diff3 markers). The lazy match swallowed part of one side's handler, leaving an unclosed block; git merged 'successfully' and main stopped compiling.
scars.mistake: Trusting any mechanical or textual conflict resolution — regex over conflict markers, 'take both sides' concatenation, or git's own clean-looking auto-merge of adjacent hunks — without a compile/parse step before committing. Textual success is not syntactic success: git only guarantees the merge had no overlapping hunks, not that the result is a program.
scars.instead: After ANY conflict resolution (manual, mechanical, or auto), run the compiler/parser as a hard gate before commit: tsc/eslint/py_compile/cargo check — bare, not piped. For 3-way append conflicts prefer `git checkout --conflict=diff3` plus manual keep-both editing over regex surgery. In CI, treat a red build immediately after a merge train as 'inspect the merge commit first'.

scars.title: Sub-package tests failed with ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND — root npm ci doesn't install nested projects
scars.summary: A repo carried an independent npm project in a subdirectory (worker/). After a fresh clone/worktree plus a root `npm ci`, the subproject's test runner crashed with ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND because its own node_modules never existed.
scars.mistake: Running `npm ci` at the repository root and assuming every test suite in the tree was now runnable. Without npm workspaces, a nested package.json (worker/, apps/*, functions/) is a separate installation unit — its devDependencies (test pool, CLI tools) are simply absent until installed in place.
scars.instead: After cloning or creating a worktree, list nested manifests first: `find . -name package.json -not -path '*/node_modules/*'`. Run `npm ci` inside each nested project before invoking its scripts. In CI, give each nested project its own install step (and its own cache key / cache-dependency-path).

scars.title: Next.js App Router component uses Client Hooks without 'use client' directive
scars.summary: Agent writes useState/useEffect/onClick in an App Router component but forgets the 'use client' directive at the top, causing build or hydration errors.
scars.mistake: Agent creates or edits an App Router component that uses useState, useEffect, onClick, or other client-side hooks and event handlers without adding 'use client' at the very first line of the file. Next.js App Router defaults all components to Server Components, so client APIs are not available. The build fails with 'You're importing a component that needs useState' or the app crashes with a hydration mismatch error at runtime.
scars.instead: Before writing any component that uses React client hooks (useState, useEffect, useRef, useCallback, useMemo, useContext) or browser event handlers (onClick, onChange, onSubmit), add 'use client' as the very first line of the file. Keep Server Components and Client Components separated — extract interactive parts into small Client Components and import them into Server Components. This minimizes the client bundle and keeps the benefits of server rendering.

scars.title: npm publish said 404 'not in this registry' — the real problem was dead auth
scars.summary: A publish failed with '404 Not Found - PUT … could not be found or you do not have permission'. It reads like a registry or package-name problem; the actual cause was an expired token (and later, an unregistered trusted publisher).
scars.mistake: Debugging the package name, registry URL, and scope after `npm publish` returned E404. npm deliberately answers unauthorized publishes with 404 instead of 401/403 so package existence is not leaked — the agent burned time on the wrong layer twice: once with a revoked automation token, once with a not-yet-registered OIDC trusted publisher.
scars.instead: On any npm publish 404/403, check auth FIRST: `npm whoami` (401 = dead token → reissue) and, for OIDC/trusted publishing, confirm the publisher is registered on the package's Settings page (org/user, repo, workflow filename, environment must match EXACTLY; the form is not validated at save time). Only debug names and registries after auth is proven.

scars.title: Two agent sessions pushed the same repo concurrently — non-fast-forward rejects and phantom state
scars.summary: One session was publishing a release while another merged a PR queue into the same repository. Pushes raced (rejected non-fast-forward), local refs moved between commands, and diffs computed seconds earlier were silently stale.
scars.mistake: Treating a repository as single-writer while multiple agents (or a human plus an agent) were pushing. The losing session saw rejected pushes, 'already up to date' answers that were false a second later, and comparisons against refs that had just moved. Worse, working directly in the same checkout meant the other session's checkouts/resets mutated the working tree mid-edit.
scars.instead: Serialize writers or isolate. (1) Claim a branch per session and work in an own `git worktree add <dir> <branch>` — shared refs still update but the working tree is yours. (2) `git fetch` immediately before any push or ref comparison, not minutes before. (3) On a rejected push never force: merge the moved remote branch into yours and push again. (4) If evidence looks impossible (stale diffs, time-traveling refs), suspect a concurrent writer before suspecting the tool.

scars.title: Two release rails fired on one tag — the downstream validator raced the upstream publisher
scars.summary: An npm-publish workflow and a registry-listing workflow both triggered on the same v* tag. The registry validates that the npm package version exists; it ran faster than the npm publish and failed with 'package not found'.
scars.mistake: Wiring two workflows to the same trigger when one of them validates the artifact the other produces. GitHub Actions gives no cross-workflow ordering — the lighter job wins the race, reads the world before the heavier job has changed it, and fails with a misleading 404/not-found that looks like a metadata bug.
scars.instead: Make the dependency explicit: either poll for the upstream artifact before proceeding (loop on `npm view <pkg> version` until it equals the tag, with a hard timeout), or chain via `workflow_run` on the upstream workflow's completion, or merge both into one workflow with `needs:`. When a downstream validator reports 'not found' right after a release, suspect the race before suspecting the metadata.

scars.title: Quality gate passed on a piped command — exit code came from the pipe tail, not the build
scars.summary: A merge was gated on `npm run build 2>&1 | tail -1` inside a && chain. The pipeline's exit status came from `tail` (0), not the compiler — a failing build passed the gate and broken code reached main.
scars.mistake: Gating a decision (merge, deploy, commit) on a piped command like `build | tail` or `test | grep`. Without pipefail the pipeline exit code is the LAST command's, so the formatter's success masks the real failure. The agent saw the chain continue, reported the gate as passed, and merged a file that did not compile.
scars.instead: Never gate on a piped command. Run the gating command bare and format its output afterwards, or `set -o pipefail` first, or capture to a variable and test `$?` explicitly. Pattern: `npm run build; rc=$?; tail -n 20 build.log; [ $rc -eq 0 ] || abort`. If you inherited a piped gate, re-run the bare command before trusting any green result.

scars.title: Prisma schema changed but `prisma generate` not run — types are stale
scars.summary: Agent modifies schema.prisma but forgets to run `npx prisma generate`, causing TypeScript errors or runtime crashes from stale client types.
scars.mistake: After editing `schema.prisma` (adding a model, changing a field), the agent runs `npx prisma db push` or `npx prisma migrate dev` to sync the DB, but forgets to run `npx prisma generate` to regenerate the TypeScript client. The code then references new fields that the Prisma Client doesn't know about, causing either TypeScript compilation errors or runtime `Unknown field` errors. Agent enters a retry loop trying to fix 'type errors' by editing the schema again.
scars.instead: After ANY change to `schema.prisma`, ALWAYS run `npx prisma generate` immediately after the migration/push command. Better yet, use `npx prisma migrate dev` which runs generate automatically. If using `db push`, chain: `npx prisma db push && npx prisma generate`. Add a `postinstall` script in package.json: `"postinstall": "prisma generate"` for CI environments.

scars.title: New scheduled automation created without inventorying existing scheduler layers — duplicated a live watcher
scars.summary: Agent adds a new recurring job (monitor/patrol/sync) after checking only one scheduler layer; an existing job on another layer already covered the work, and a previously DISABLED twin encoded a deliberate decision the new job silently reversed.
scars.mistake: Asked to set up monitoring, the agent designed and created a new daily scheduled job after checking only the layer it was working in. A 4-hourly job on a different layer (OS timer vs cloud scheduler vs CI cron) already covered a superset of the checks — stateful, with a working notification path. Worse, a disabled twin of the new job existed: the owner had deliberately turned daily runs off, and the new job resurrected that cost. The new job was also stateless with a frozen baseline, so any legitimate change (a version bump, a new PR) would alarm forever. Options were even presented to the owner BEFORE the inventory, so they chose on false premises.
scars.instead: Before creating ANY recurring automation, inventory every scheduler layer the machine/org actually uses: OS level (crontab, systemd list-timers --user AND --system), agent-platform schedulers (cloud routines / scheduled agents), and CI scheduled workflows. Then search project notes for prior decisions in that domain — a disabled twin is a recorded decision, not free real estate. Prefer extending the existing stateful watcher with missing checks over adding a parallel stateless one (frozen-baseline monitors produce permanent false alarms). If you ask the owner to choose an architecture, run the inventory FIRST so the options are real.

scars.title: Stripe webhook signature verification fails — body already parsed as JSON
scars.summary: Agent reads the webhook body with `request.json()` before verifying the signature, causing `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent` to fail with 'No signatures found matching the expected signature for payload'.
scars.mistake: In the webhook handler, the agent calls `await request.json()` to read the body, then passes the parsed object to `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent()`. But constructEvent needs the RAW string body, not a parsed JSON object. The signature is computed against the raw bytes — parsing and re-stringifying changes whitespace/ordering, so the signature never matches. The agent retries by changing the webhook secret, regenerating keys, or rewriting the entire handler — none of which fix the actual problem.
scars.instead: Use `await request.text()` (NOT `request.json()`) to get the raw body string. Pass that raw string directly to `stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(rawBody, sig, webhookSecret)`. In Next.js App Router, `request.text()` returns the untouched body by default — no configuration needed. ONLY parse the JSON AFTER signature verification succeeds: `const event = JSON.parse(rawBody)` or use the event returned by constructEvent.

scars.title: Supabase table created without Row Level Security — all data publicly accessible
scars.summary: Agent creates a Supabase table without enabling RLS, leaving every row readable and writable by any anonymous user through the public API.
scars.mistake: Agent runs CREATE TABLE in Supabase without following up with ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY and adding policies. Supabase exposes tables via the public REST API by default. Without RLS, the anon key can read, insert, update, and delete every row in the table. The agent sees 'table created' and moves on, unaware that the entire dataset is now publicly exposed to anyone with the project URL.
scars.instead: Immediately after every CREATE TABLE statement, run ALTER TABLE <table_name> ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. Then add at least one RLS policy that restricts access based on auth.uid() or auth.role(). For example: CREATE POLICY "Users can read own data" ON <table_name> FOR SELECT USING (auth.uid() = user_id). Verify RLS is active by checking the Supabase dashboard or running SELECT relrowsecurity FROM pg_class WHERE relname = '<table_name>'. Never leave a table without RLS in any Supabase project.

scars.title: Tailwind v4 broken because PostCSS plugin is set to 'tailwindcss' instead of '@tailwindcss/postcss'
scars.summary: Using the v3 PostCSS plugin name in a v4 project causes styles to silently fail or produce build errors.
scars.mistake: Setting the PostCSS plugin to `tailwindcss` in `postcss.config.mjs` when the project uses Tailwind CSS v4. This is the v3 plugin name. In v4, the PostCSS plugin was renamed to `@tailwindcss/postcss`. The build may not error — it may silently fall back to v3 behavior or produce no utility classes at all. The agent sees no build error and reports success, but the styles are broken.
scars.instead: Always check the installed Tailwind version (`node_modules/tailwindcss/package.json`). For v4+, the PostCSS config must use `@tailwindcss/postcss` as the plugin, not `tailwindcss`. Install with `npm install -D @tailwindcss/postcss`. Also replace `@tailwind base; @tailwind components; @tailwind utilities;` with `@import "tailwindcss"` in the CSS entry point, and remove `autoprefixer` from PostCSS plugins (v4 includes it).

scars.title: CI/API timestamps are UTC — read as local time, events were misdated by a day
scars.summary: gh/CI output printed 15:2x UTC; the local clock said 00:2x next day. The agent concluded a merge was 'yesterday' when it happened minutes earlier, and nearly misdiagnosed live concurrent activity as history.
scars.mistake: Reasoning about recency ('this ran yesterday', 'nothing is active now') directly from raw timestamps in gh run list, API responses, or CI logs. Those are UTC; for a UTC+9 operator every evening event lands on the previous calendar date, so causality and concurrency judgments come out wrong.
scars.instead: Convert to local time before any recency or ordering claim (UTC+offset; JST = UTC+9). Heuristic: if timeline conclusions look about 9 hours or one day off from expectations, check the timezone first. Prefer sources that render locally: `git log --date=iso-local`, `date -d '<ts>'`. State timestamps with their zone in reports.

scars.title: Vercel deploy succeeds but app crashes — missing env vars
scars.summary: Build passes because env vars aren't required at build time, but the app crashes at runtime because they're not set in Vercel's environment settings.
scars.mistake: Deploying to Vercel without checking that all `process.env.*` variables used in the app are configured in Vercel's Environment Variables dashboard. The build succeeds because Next.js doesn't fail on missing env vars at build time (they're just `undefined`). The agent sees 'deploy successful' and reports done. The user opens the URL and gets a 500 error.
scars.instead: Before running `vercel --prod`, run `vercel env ls` and cross-reference with every `process.env.*` in the codebase. For each missing variable, run `vercel env add <NAME>`. Also check `.env.local` and `.env.production` for any vars that exist locally but not on Vercel. Never assume 'build passed = app works'.

scars.title: Vitest cannot resolve @/ imports because path aliases do not match tsconfig.json
scars.summary: Tests fail with 'Cannot find module @/...' because vitest.config.ts resolve.alias does not mirror tsconfig.json paths.
scars.mistake: Setting up Vitest without replicating the project's tsconfig.json `compilerOptions.paths` in `vitest.config.ts` `resolve.alias`. The agent creates vitest.config.ts with no alias, or with a hardcoded `@` -> `./src` alias without checking what tsconfig.json actually says. Tests then fail with `Cannot find module '@/components/...'` or similar. This is the number one cause of Vitest setup failure in Next.js projects.
scars.instead: Before writing vitest.config.ts, read tsconfig.json `compilerOptions.paths` and replicate EVERY alias entry in `resolve.alias`. For example, if tsconfig has `"@/*": ["./src/*"]`, vitest must have `alias: { '@': path.resolve(__dirname, './src') }`. If tsconfig has additional aliases like `"@db/*": ["./src/db/*"]`, those must also be added. After creating the config, run `npx vitest run` to verify the aliases resolve correctly.

scars.title: Converting photos to WebP without measuring can produce larger, worse files
scars.summary: Agent assumes 'WebP is smaller than JPEG', converts a grainy-texture photo, and gets a file that is larger at equal quality and uglier at equal size.
scars.mistake: Following the general rule that WebP/AVIF beat JPEG, the agent converts a hero photo with strong grain/noise texture (paper, film, marble) to WebP. At q80 the WebP is ~1.5x LARGER than the optimized JPEG; even at q55 it is still not smaller and visibly degraded. High-frequency noise compresses poorly in WebP's prediction model. The agent nearly ships a <picture> setup that increases transfer size while adding markup complexity.
scars.instead: Never switch image formats on faith: encode the actual asset at 2-3 quality levels and compare bytes AND visual quality against the optimized original before adopting. For noisy/grainy photos, prefer keeping a well-compressed JPEG and win transfer size with srcset width steps (e.g. 900w for mobile, full width for desktop) plus correct sizes. Record the measured numbers in the commit/README so the decision is auditable.

scars.title: Shipped the write path, never wired the read path — the feature recorded data nobody ever read back
scars.summary: A failure-memory feature stored records perfectly, but no query path ever loaded them: lookups and reports ran only against the built-in corpus. The product's core loop was silently open for a full release cycle while all write-side tests were green.
scars.mistake: Verifying a data feature by testing only the write side (record succeeds, file exists, schema valid) and declaring it done. Nothing asserted that a consumer actually loads what was written, so the recall half was never wired: recorded data was invisible to every read endpoint, and the gap survived reviews because each half looked complete on its own.
scars.instead: For every write path, name the read path in the same change and prove the loop with one end-to-end test: write through the public API, then assert the written record is visible through each consumer that should see it (query, report, recall). Audit question that catches this class in review: 'who reads this, and where is the test that reads it?' Also verify the loop in the PRODUCTION wiring (the binary/config actually deployed), not just in unit scope.
